def_en: a great victory or achievement; a public celebration of success
def_zh: in triumph, triumphant return, triumph over
quote_en: "Love in fantastic triumph sat, / Whilst bleeding hearts around him flowed" – The speaker personifies Love as a victorious general surrounded by the casualties of his conquest (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn opens with 'triumph' to frame Love not as a tender cherub but as a conquering warlord revelling in destruction. The word is sharply ironic: a Roman 'triumph' honoured a general returning from justified war, yet this victor sits 'fantastic' — a word suggestive of delusion and theatrical excess. The juxtaposition with 'bleeding hearts' in the very next line undercuts any sense of glory. Hearts should be the seat of affection, yet here they are wounds, flowing around him in a grotesque parody of a triumphal procession. Behn draws on the Petrarchan tradition of the heart as battlefield but radicalises it: this is not a lover's complaint but a political indictment. 'Triumph' establishes the poem's governing metaphor of love as warfare and its central moral question — who pays the cost of one person's victory?
ex_en: Love sat celebrating a military triumph while the wounded hearts of his victims bled around him.
ex_zh: You wore your triumph like a crown / of birthday candles, lit and dripping — / and everyone applauded / as the wax burned into your scalp.

def_en: exercising power in a cruel, oppressive, or arbitrary way; characteristic of a tyrant
def_zh: tyrannic rule, tyrannic power, tyrannic grip
quote_en: "For whom fresh pains he did create, / And strange tyrannic power he showed." – The speaker continues describing Love's cruel governance over those already wounded (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn selects 'tyrannic' over milder alternatives such as 'cruel' or 'harsh' to invoke a specifically political register. The word carried seventeenth-century resonances of absolute monarchy and the abuse of sovereign power — a charged term in the decades following the English Civil War. 'Tyrannic' transforms Love from a mythological figure into a ruler who governs without consent. Coupled with 'strange', it suggests an unfamiliar, almost incomprehensible cruelty: this is not ordinary suffering but a perversion of legitimate authority. The adjective also anticipates the poem's final revelation that both lovers have 'set him up a deity' — they are complicit in arming the very tyrant who oppresses them. Behn uses 'tyrannic' to collapse the distance between political critique and erotic lament, insisting that the private sphere of love replicates the structures of public tyranny.
ex_en: Love demonstrated a strange and tyrannic authority by inventing new torments for those already bleeding at his feet.
ex_zh: My father's tyrannic silence / ruled the dinner table for decades — / we learned to chew quietly, / to swallow our words before they hatched.

def_en: the visible effect of combustion; burning passion or strong emotion
def_zh: took fire, eyes of fire, fire hurled
quote_en: "From thy bright eyes he took his fire, / Which round about in sport he hurled" – The speaker explains that Love stole the beloved's radiant glance and turned it into a weapon scattered carelessly (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn's 'fire' operates on multiple planes simultaneously. In the Petrarchan sonnet tradition, the beloved's eyes conventionally emit burning darts of love. Behn acknowledges this convention but sharpens it: the fire is not emitted willingly by the beloved but 'took' by Love — a theft that renders the beloved's beauty raw material for weaponry. The verb 'hurled' intensifies the violence; this is not a gentle scattering but a forceful, almost reckless throwing. The phrase 'in sport' is devastating in its casualness — Love treats conflagration as a game. Phonetically, 'fire' burns brightly against the softer sounds of 'thee' and 'thine' that dominate the stanza, its fricative and liquid consonants demanding the reader's attention. The word initiates the poem's catalogue of stolen attributes, positioning Love as a parasite who produces nothing himself but appropriates everything from those he victimises.
ex_en: Love stole the fire from the beloved's bright eyes and threw it carelessly in all directions as if playing a game.
ex_zh: She carried fire in her coat pocket — / not matches, just the memory / of the look he gave her / that set the whole school corridor ablaze.

def_en: to unfasten or untie; to reverse or cancel; to bring to ruin or destruction
def_zh: undo the world, undo a knot, come undone
quote_en: "But 'twas from mine he took desire / Enough to undo the amorous world." – The speaker reveals that her own desire fuels Love's power to unravel everything love holds together (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn chooses 'undo' with devastating precision. A more neutral verb like 'destroy' or 'ruin' would convey destruction, but 'undo' carries the specific sense of reversal — of taking apart something that was once whole. The word echoes the language of untying knots or unweaving fabric, suggesting that the 'amorous world' is a delicate construction that can be picked apart thread by thread. The modifier 'Enough' intensifies the tragedy: the speaker's desire is not merely destructive but sufficient in quantity to dismantle an entire universe of love. There is a dark algebra at work — one woman's longing equals the unmaking of everything. The enjambment across the couplet (lines 7–8) enacts this process of undoing at the level of form, as the sentence threatens to spill beyond the neat boundaries of the heroic couplet. Behn positions the female speaker's desire as paradoxically generative and annihilating: it arms Love but unmakes the world Love is supposed to sustain.
ex_en: Love took from the speaker a desire powerful enough to undo and unravel the entire world of love.
ex_zh: One summer unstitched itself slowly — / an undone button on a blouse, / an undone promise on the porch, / an undone family by September.

def_en: behaviour that causes pain or suffering to others; callous indifference to suffering
def_zh: pride and cruelty, act of cruelty, casual cruelty
quote_en: "From me he took his sighs and tears, / From thee his pride and cruelty" – The speaker catalogues how Love assembled his nature from two unequal sources: her vulnerability, the beloved's arrogance (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: 'Cruelty' arrives at the structural centre of the poem as the harshest word in Behn's lexicon of suffering. It is the only abstract noun in the catalogue that names a moral failing rather than an emotion. 'Sighs', 'tears', 'languishments', and 'fears' all describe internal states of the sufferer, but 'cruelty' — like 'pride', with which it is paired — describes the active infliction of pain by one person upon another. Behn assigns 'cruelty' to the 'thee' — the beloved — not to Love. This is crucial: Love is armed with cruelty borrowed from the beloved. The alliterative coupling 'pride and cruelty' binds these two sins together sonically, as if pride naturally hardens into cruelty across the caesura. In the asymmetrical architecture of the poem, the speaker contributes only vulnerabilities while the beloved contributes only weapons. 'Cruelty' thus exposes the injustice at the heart of the love relationship: one party supplies the suffering, the other the means to inflict it.
ex_en: Love took sighs and tears from the speaker, but took pride and cruelty from the beloved.
ex_zh: She taught me cruelty as a language — / how to turn "I miss you" into a full stop, / how to leave a message on read / and call it punctuation.

def_en: causing death; lethal; overwhelming in effect
def_zh: killing dart, killing blow, killing glance
quote_en: "From me his languishments and fears, / And every killing dart from thee." – The speaker concludes the catalogue: her inner torments, the beloved's deadly weapons (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn reserves 'killing' for the climactic position in the poem's central catalogue. The word transforms the conventional Cupid's dart — traditionally a sweet, piercing agent of love — into a lethal projectile. The present participle form is significant: 'killing' is ongoing, durative, not a single completed act but a continuous process of annihilation. The modifier 'every' reinforces this relentlessness; there is no single fatal blow but an endless succession of killing darts, each one drawn from the beloved. Phonetically, the harsh plosive /k/ of 'killing' echoes 'cruelty' from the previous couplet, creating a percussive pattern of violence through the poem's soundscape. The word also carries a submerged pun: in seventeenth-century usage, 'killing' could mean irresistible or overwhelming in an erotic sense. Behn exploits both meanings — the beloved's darts are both murderous and devastatingly attractive, collapsing the distinction between seduction and destruction.
ex_en: Every killing dart that Love threw came from the beloved, while the speaker contributed only exhaustion and fear.
ex_zh: She had a killing way of being kind — / her compliments arrived like ambulances / that looked like they were racing to save you / but somehow always pronounced you dead.

def_en: equipped with weapons; supplied with the means of attack or defence
def_zh: armed a god, fully armed, armed and ready
quote_en: "Thus thou and I the god have armed, / And set him up a deity" – The speaker concludes that both lovers are complicit in equipping Love with his destructive power (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: 'Armed' is the poem's title word and its pivotal revelation. Behn delays it until the penultimate stanza, forcing the reader to witness the effects of Love's weaponry before understanding its source. The verb implicates both 'thou' and 'I' as joint agents: they are not passive victims but active armourers. The word draws together every weapon image that precedes it — fire, darts, killing — and names their common origin in human complicity. 'Armed' also completes the poem's military lexicon ('triumph', 'tyrannic', 'power', 'hurled', 'dart', 'killing'), revealing that the entire poem has been a martial allegory of a love affair. In the political context of the 1670s, 'armed' carried resonance with debates about standing armies and royal power, but Behn applies it to the domestic sphere, insisting that the politics of love are no less consequential than the politics of state. The couplet's syntax makes 'the god' the object of 'have armed' — the lovers have made Love into what he is. This is a radical theological claim: deities are not born powerful; they are armed by their worshippers.
ex_en: The beloved and the speaker together armed the god of Love with weapons and elevated him to divine status.
ex_zh: We armed our silences with everything we had — / teaspoons, car keys, old photographs — / until the kitchen became an arsenal / and nobody remembered what started the war.

def_en: a person who defeats an enemy or opponent in a battle, contest, or struggle
def_zh: the victor is, emerge victor, victor and vanquished
quote_en: "But my poor heart alone is harmed, / Whilst thine the victor is, and free." – The speaker reveals the bitter asymmetry of love's aftermath: she is wounded, the beloved triumphant and liberated (Song: Love Armed, 1676)
quote_zh: Behn closes with 'victor', a word that completes the poem's cyclical martial frame by echoing the opening 'triumph'. A triumph was the ceremonial honour granted to a victor in ancient Rome; the poem thus ends where it began, with conquest — but the final couplet radically revises the earlier image. The victor is no longer Love himself but the beloved, who emerges 'free' from the conflict that has 'harmed' the speaker. The stark antithesis between 'poor heart' and 'victor', between 'harmed' and 'free', enacts the fundamental injustice the poem has catalogued. 'Victor' carries connotations of legitimacy — victors are celebrated, their violence retrospectively justified — yet Behn exposes the moral vacancy beneath the title. The beloved's victory is parasitic, built entirely on weapons Love stole from both parties. The word's final position, rhyming with 'free', leaves the reader with the uncomfortable recognition that in love, as in war, one person's freedom can be purchased with another's pain.
ex_en: The speaker's heart alone was harmed, while the beloved's heart remained the victor, unharmed and free.
ex_zh: You left as the victor always leaves — / without looking back at the rubble, / your shadow the only flag still flying / over a country I no longer recognise.

def_en: left one's own country in order to settle permanently in another
def_zh: emigrated to, family emigrated, simply emigrated
quote_en: "Great Pan is not dead; / he simply emigrated / to India." – The speaker revises the classical myth, asserting that the Greek god Pan did not die but relocated and now lives in India (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt opens with 'emigrated', a startlingly modern, bureaucratic verb applied to an ancient god. The expected word in the Pan myth is 'dead' — Plutarch records the cry that 'Great Pan is dead' as marking the end of the pagan gods. Bhatt replaces death with migration, refusing the narrative of endings and replacing it with one of movement and survival. 'Emigrated' is a word of passports, borders, and displacement; it humanises Pan while also suggesting the historical movement of peoples and cultures across continents. The adverb 'simply' is quietly subversive — as if the god's relocation from Arcadia to India were the most natural thing in the world. The word establishes the poem's governing logic: nothing sacred is ever truly lost; it merely moves, transforms, and takes root elsewhere. This idea prepares the ground for the poem's later meditation on how language and beauty survive colonisation.
ex_en: The god Pan did not die but emigrated from Greece to live in India instead.
ex_zh: The gods emigrated quietly one Tuesday — / left their marble postcodes for dustier addresses, / learned new alphabets, / and started writing home in Sanskrit.

def_en: having altered one's appearance to conceal identity; hidden behind a false appearance
def_zh: disguised as, heavily disguised, disguised identity
quote_en: "Here, the gods roam freely, / disguised as snakes or monkeys;" – The speaker describes how deities in India move through the world hidden in animal forms (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt chooses 'disguised' over softer alternatives like 'appearing' or 'manifesting' to emphasise concealment and the gap between surface and truth. A disguise implies something hidden that requires recognition — the divine is present but not obvious. The word also carries a faintly mischievous quality: gods in disguise are tricksters, crossing boundaries, evading easy identification. The animals named — snakes and monkeys — are both quotidian and sacred in the Hindu tradition (the monkey god Hanuman, the serpent associated with Shiva). By pairing the cosmic with the ordinary, 'disguised' suggests that the sacred does not announce itself with trumpets but moves quietly through the everyday. The free indirect structure 'roam freely' reinforces this sense of liberty: unlike the fixed, statuesque gods of Europe, these deities wander at will. Bhatt implicitly contrasts this living, shapeshifting divinity with the dead or absent gods of the West.
ex_en: The gods moved through India freely but in disguise, taking the forms of ordinary snakes and monkeys.
ex_zh: Grief learned to walk among us disguised / as politeness, / nodding at neighbours, / asking for the recipe one more time.

def_en: connected with God or a god; regarded as holy and deserving of veneration
def_zh: sacred tree, sacred text, considered sacred
quote_en: "every tree is sacred / and it is a sin / to be rude to a book." – The speaker equates the sanctity of nature with the sanctity of knowledge, linking trees and books in shared holiness (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt uses 'sacred' to fuse two systems of reverence that Western thought typically separates: the natural world and the written word. The word operates as a bridge, implying that the same spiritual obligation that protects a tree also protects a book made from that tree. This is not metaphor but literal connection — the tree from whose wood paper was made (revealed later in line 18) physically links forest to text. 'Sacred' also establishes a contrast with the poem's later treatment of language as an instrument of oppression. In this first half of the poem, books are holy objects; in the second half, the coloniser's tongue is a 'dying river'. The tension between these two visions of language — as sacred vessel and as weapon — drives the poem's central argument. Bhatt refuses to resolve this tension, insisting that reverence and violence can coexist in the same linguistic tradition.
ex_en: Every tree in India is treated as sacred, and therefore mistreating a book is considered a sin.
ex_zh: The sacred survived the fire oddly — / not in the prayer books, / but in the way my grandmother / still washed rice three times before cooking.

def_en: an act regarded as a transgression against divine law; a serious moral offence
def_zh: a sin to, commit a sin, sin against
quote_en: "It is a sin to shove a book aside / with your foot, / a sin to slam books down hard / on a table, / a sin to toss one carelessly / across a room." – The speaker recites a litany of forbidden acts, all involving disrespect toward books (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: The anaphoric repetition of 'sin' across six lines creates an incantatory rhythm that mimics religious instruction. Bhatt chooses 'sin' rather than 'rude', 'wrong', or 'disrespectful' to elevate the mistreatment of books to a theological offence. The word carries gravity, fear, and the weight of divine judgment. Each prohibition is intensely physical: shoving with a foot, slamming, tossing. These are bodily sins, committed through gesture and carelessness. Bhatt implies that reverence is not primarily intellectual but somatic — it lives in how one handles objects, how one moves through space. The list structure also echoes the colonial schoolroom, where rules were drilled into children through repetition. Yet here the rules are not European but rooted in Hindu and Indian traditions of respect for knowledge. 'Sin' reappropriates a Christian term for a non-Christian context, subtly decentring Western religious vocabulary and insisting that the sacred is not geographically confined.
ex_en: It is a sin to treat books carelessly, whether by pushing them with your foot, slamming them down, or throwing them across a room.
ex_zh: The smallest sin I ever committed / was leaving your letter unopened for three days — / the paper grew colder each morning, / until the words inside froze to silence.

def_en: in a careful, tender, or mild manner; without force or harshness
def_zh: turn gently, speak gently, gently does it
quote_en: "You must learn how to turn the pages gently / without disturbing Sarasvati, / without offending the tree / from whose wood the paper was made." – The speaker gives positive instruction on the correct, reverent way to handle a book (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: 'Gently' is the poem's sole adverb of instruction and the positive counterpart to the litany of 'sin' that precedes it. Where the prohibitions told the reader what not to do, 'gently' models what to do — a tender, attentive physical practice. Bhatt chooses a word that applies equally to handling a fragile object and to interacting with a living being. This doubleness is crucial: the pages must be turned gently not only to preserve the paper but to avoid 'disturbing Sarasvati', the Hindu goddess of knowledge, music, and the arts. The goddess is imagined as a presence within the book, capable of being woken or offended. 'Gently' thus carries theological weight — it is a form of worship performed through the fingertips. The adverb also bridges the two justifications given in the following lines: one religious (Sarasvati), one ecological (the tree). Both demand the same quality of attention, and 'gently' supplies it in a single, quiet syllable.
ex_en: You must learn to turn the pages gently so that you neither disturb the goddess of knowledge nor offend the tree that gave its wood for the paper.
ex_zh: She learned to leave gently — / no slammed doors, no final words — / just a coat taken slowly from its hook / and the soft click of a latch that understood.

def_en: a person or group that rules over others in a harsh and unfair way; a tyrant
def_zh: oppressor's tongue, oppressor and oppressed, voice of the oppressor
quote_en: "Which language / has not been the oppressor's tongue? / Which language / truly meant to murder someone?" – The speaker pivots to a rhetorical question about the inherent violence in all languages of power (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt deploys 'oppressor' with the force of a verdict. The word is unflinching, political, and collective — it personifies language itself as an agent of domination. The epithet 'oppressor's tongue' fuses two ideas: the physical organ of speech and the linguistic system imposed by colonial power. The rhetorical question that frames it ('Which language has not been...') universalises the accusation. Bhatt refuses to single out English alone; every language that has ever held power has been an instrument of coercion. This is a striking move in a poem that might easily have become a simple indictment of British colonialism. 'Oppressor' also introduces the poem's ethical complexity: if all languages are guilty, how can one choose between them? How can the same tongue that produced 'coral-reef' also produce torture? The word forces the reader to sit with the uncomfortable truth that beauty and violence can issue from the same mouth.
ex_en: The speaker asks which language in all of human history has not at some point been the voice of an oppressor used to control and harm others.
ex_zh: The oppressor lived in my mouth for years — / I fed it grammar, gave it vowels, / and only realised its cruelty / when it started translating my mother's songs into silence.

def_en: continues to live or exist, especially in spite of danger or hardship
def_zh: survives after, still survives, only survives
quote_en: "And how does it happen / that after the torture, / after the soul has been cropped out / of a human being, / a word like 'wide-sargasso-sea' / or 'coral-reef' survives" – The speaker marvels at how a beautiful word endures after the violent erasure of a people's soul (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: 'Survives' is the poem's central verb and its deepest question. Bhatt places it at the heart of an extended syntactic suspension — the sentence stretches across nine lines before reaching this main verb, enacting grammatically the endurance the word describes. The choice of 'survives' over 'remains' or 'endures' is significant: survival implies threat, danger, the real possibility of having been destroyed. The word carries the weight of the 'torture' and 'soul-cropping' that precede it. What survives is not a whole culture or an intact tradition but fragments — a hyphenated compound, a borrowed image. 'Wide-sargasso-sea' and 'coral-reef' are both drawn from the natural world and from the coloniser's lexicon (Jean Rhys's novel, colonial geography). Bhatt does not offer patriotic answers; the beauty that survives is impure, hybrid, drawn from the very tradition that inflicted the violence. 'Survives' is thus neither triumphant nor consolatory — it is simply, stubbornly, astonishingly true.
ex_en: The speaker asks how beautiful words from the coloniser's language survive even after that same language has been used to strip a person of their soul.
ex_zh: The song survived in the wrong key, / with half the verses missing, / but the chorus still rose every evening / from kitchens that had forgotten how to dance.

def_en: a dense growth of shrubs and other plants, especially under trees; the thick, tangled lower layer of vegetation
def_zh: struggling undergrowth, thick undergrowth, tangled undergrowth
quote_en: "working its way through the struggling flesh / of the undergrowth, / thick and tangled as it is?" – The speaker extends the botanical metaphor, imagining the surviving word as something pushing through dense, resistant vegetation (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt chooses 'undergrowth' to describe the cultural and psychological terrain through which a transplanted word must struggle. The word is richly ambiguous: it suggests both the literal vegetation of a colonised landscape and the inner tangle of a colonised consciousness. 'Undergrowth' is what grows beneath the canopy, out of sight, often dismissed as weeds or brush. By dignifying it with attention, Bhatt reverses the colonial gaze that saw only 'jungle' or 'wilderness' where indigenous cultures flourished. The adjective 'struggling' applied to 'flesh' of the undergrowth creates a startling personification — the landscape itself is alive, suffering, and resisting. 'Thick and tangled' suggests density that is neither good nor bad but simply real, the texture of lived experience that cannot be smoothed into colonial order. The word also echoes the poem's earlier image of the tree that becomes paper; here the tree fights back, its undergrowth a resistant network that slows and transforms whatever passes through it.
ex_en: The transplanted word pushes slowly through the dense and tangled undergrowth of a damaged culture, struggling to find a place to root.
ex_zh: The undergrowth remembers everything the canopy forgets — / the fallen nests, the buried letters, / the small bones of songs / that never learned to fly.

def_en: the flower of the mango tree, known for its sweet, warm fragrance; the bloom that precedes the fruit
def_zh: smell of mango-blossom, golden mango-blossom, scent of mango-blossom
quote_en: "and the whole room fills / with the warm, sweet, almost golden / smell of mango-blossom" – The speaker describes how a young man's memory of his father's tree becomes a sensory presence so powerful it saturates the air (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt's 'mango-blossom' is the poem's moment of pure sensory plenitude, the counterweight to all the violence and loss catalogued elsewhere. The hyphenated compound combines fruit (mango) with potential (blossom), suggesting something poised between promise and fulfilment. The word is intensely specific to the Indian landscape — no European poet could reach for 'mango-blossom' as an image of home. In reclaiming it, Bhatt performs the very act of cultural survival the poem theorises. The modifiers accumulate in a crescendo: 'warm', 'sweet', 'almost golden' — the 'almost' is perfect, acknowledging the inadequacy of language to capture sensory memory while still reaching for it. The synaesthetic merging of smell and colour ('golden') blurs the senses as intense memory does. Crucially, this fragrance is triggered by a young man thinking of a tree his father planted — memory is generational, rooted in lineage and cultivation, not in abstract nationalism. 'Mango-blossom' is what the oppressor's tongue cannot extinguish.
ex_en: When a young man remembers the mango tree his father planted, the entire room fills with the sweet golden scent of mango-blossom.
ex_zh: Mango-blossom arrives before the fruit, / a promise written in pollen — / my father's father planted the tree, / and I am still waiting for the sweetness to fall.

def_en: fine, dry particles of matter; the powdery residue of something that has disintegrated
def_zh: dust of language, bitter dust, electric dust
quote_en: "and the dust of his language, / the sharp, piercing, bitter, electric dust / of his half-forgotten mother tongue / sits on the tip of his tongue / and refuses to go away" – The speaker describes the persistent, irritating residue of a language almost lost but never entirely gone (A Different History, 1988)
quote_zh: Bhatt closes with 'dust', the most paradoxical and haunting word in the poem. Dust is what remains after destruction — the fine particulate of something once whole. A 'half-forgotten mother tongue' reduced to dust suggests cultural loss, attrition, the grinding down of a language by the machinery of colonisation. Yet Bhatt's dust is not dead matter. The string of adjectives — 'sharp, piercing, bitter, electric' — charges it with almost unbearable intensity. 'Electric' is the shock: dust that carries current, that can spark and jolt. The word transforms residue into resistance. The physical placement of this dust 'on the tip of his tongue' is excruciatingly precise — it is right there, almost speakable, almost recoverable, but not quite. The verb 'sits' suggests stubborn patience, and 'refuses to go away' closes the poem on an act of defiance. Dust is what will not be swept clean, what settles in the cracks and waits. Against the grand narratives of colonial history, Bhatt offers this small, gritty, irreducible remainder.
ex_en: The sharp bitter dust of the young man's half-forgotten mother tongue sits on the tip of his tongue and stubbornly refuses to disappear.
ex_zh: The dust of everything we never said / settled on the windowsill in layers — / I wrote my name in it once, / and the letters grew roots overnight.

def_en: exchanged for money; transferred ownership of a person or thing for payment
def_zh: sold me, sold into, bought and sold
quote_en: "When my mother died I was very young, / And my father sold me while yet my tongue" – The child speaker recounts how, after his mother's death, his father sold him into chimney-sweeping while he was still barely able to speak (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake opens with 'sold', a verb of brutal economic transaction that transforms a child into property. The word is stark, monosyllabic, and unsoftened by any qualifier — the father does not 'send' or 'apprentice' or 'give' the boy; he sells him. In the context of late eighteenth-century London, this was not metaphor but legal reality: poor children were routinely sold into chimney-sweeping by desperate families. 'Sold' also establishes the poem's moral framework. The speaker states it without visible anger, in the flat tone of a child who accepts his commodification as a fact of life, which makes the word all the more devastating. The enjambment that carries the sentence across the line break — 'while yet my tongue / Could scarcely cry' — enacts the violence: the sale happens mid-utterance, as if language itself is interrupted by commerce. The word silently indicts not only the father but the society that made such transactions possible, including the 'your' addressed directly in line four.
ex_en: The boy's father sold him to be a chimney-sweeper when he was so young he could barely speak.
ex_zh: My childhood was sold in a hallway / to a man with clean fingernails / who counted out banknotes / while I was still learning the word for no.

def_en: to shed tears; to cry, especially from grief or pain
def_zh: weep weep, cry and weep, weep for
quote_en: "Could scarcely cry " 'weep! 'weep! 'weep! 'weep!" / So your chimneys I sweep & in soot I sleep." – The child speaker reproduces his own lisping street-cry, a sound that mingles the call of his trade with the act of weeping (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'weep is a devastating pun that collapses labour and grief into a single syllable. Literally, the child is attempting the street-cry 'Sweep! Sweep!' — the call of the chimney-sweep advertising his trade to householders. But his infant tongue, too young for the consonant cluster, produces ''weep'' instead. The apostrophe marks the absent 's', the missing sound that betrays the child's age and vulnerability. Blake exploits the fact that a chimney-sweep's work-cry and a child's cry of distress are phonetically and emotionally inseparable. The quadruple repetition — ''weep! 'weep! 'weep! 'weep!'' — is both the rhythm of the street-hawker and the sound of inconsolable sobbing. The word also foreshadows the poem's later vision of liberation: weeping is the waking reality, while joy exists only in dreams. By placing this pun in the child's own voice, Blake avoids sentimentality and achieves something harder — the sound of a child too young to know his own tragedy.
ex_en: The young chimney-sweeper's street-cry sounded like the word 'weep', as he called for work while also expressing his misery.
ex_zh: The word wept before it left my mouth — / I had meant to say something brave / but the vowels flooded their banks / and the consonants drowned in the shallows.

def_en: a young sheep; figuratively, a gentle, innocent, or defenceless person
def_zh: like a lamb, sacrificial lamb, lamb's back
quote_en: "There's little Tom Dacre, who cried when his head / That curled like a lamb's back, was shaved, so I said," – The speaker introduces his fellow sweep Tom, whose soft curling hair resembled the fleece of a young lamb before it was shorn (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'lamb' carries enormous symbolic weight from his wider poetic universe. The lamb, in Songs of Innocence, is the emblem of Christ, gentleness, and divine protection. To describe Tom's curls as 'like a lamb's back' is to invoke this entire symbolic framework in a single comparison. The shaving of the hair becomes a ritual sacrifice — the lamb is shorn, the innocent is marked for suffering. Yet the simile also softens the violence: a lamb's wool is shorn to be used, and it grows back. The child's hair, by implication, is a natural resource harvested by those more powerful. The word also connects Tom to the sweeps' working conditions: like lambs to the slaughter, these children are led into chimneys that will damage and kill them. Blake's choice of 'lamb' rather than 'sheep' emphasises youth — a lamb is a baby animal, small and vulnerable, unable to protect itself.
ex_en: The boy Tom Dacre cried when they shaved off his hair, which had curled softly like the back of a young lamb.
ex_zh: The lamb in him survived the shearing — / each year the wool grew back softer, / until the farmer stopped sharpening his blades / and learned to keep warm without taking anything.

def_en: of the colour of snow or milk; pure, innocent, or unsullied
def_zh: white hair, pure white, white as
quote_en: ""Hush, Tom! never mind it, for when your head's bare, / You know that the soot cannot spoil your white hair."" – The speaker consoles Tom by pointing out that with his head shaved, the black soot can no longer ruin the colour of his pale hair (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'white' operates in the poem's visual and moral colour scheme as the opposite of soot, darkness, and corruption. The child logic of the consolation is heartbreakingly practical: white hair cannot be spoiled by black soot if there is no hair left to spoil. But 'white' also carries the symbolic freight of innocence familiar from Songs of Innocence. White is the colour of the lamb, of the naked children in the dream-vision, of the Angel and the clouds. In a poem obsessed with blackness — soot, coffins, dark mornings — 'white' is the quality constantly under threat and yet, in the dream at least, miraculously restored. The irony the child speaker cannot voice is that the shaving itself is a violence, a pre-emptive mutilation offered as comfort. Blake uses 'white' to expose how innocence is brutalised in the name of preserving it. The soot cannot spoil the white hair because the white hair has already been taken away.
ex_en: The speaker told Tom not to cry because with his head shaved bare, the black soot would not be able to dirty his pale white hair.
ex_zh: She kept her white dress folded in a drawer / for a day that never came — / it yellowed quietly, like milk / that forgot the taste of morning.

def_en: something seen; a vision, spectacle, or remarkable display
def_zh: had a sight, strange sight, wonderful sight
quote_en: "And so he was quiet, & that very night, / As Tom was a-sleeping he had such a sight!" – The speaker describes how Tom, having been consoled, fell asleep and experienced a powerful dream-vision (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake chooses 'sight' over alternatives like 'dream' or 'vision' to emphasise the visual, almost supernatural clarity of Tom's experience. In Blake's poetic vocabulary, 'sight' carries prophetic weight — visions and revelations are seen, not merely imagined. The word also distances the experience from mere sleep: Tom does not simply dream; he is granted a sight, a revelation that carries moral and spiritual authority. The exclamation mark at the end of the couplet signals the speaker's excitement, a rare moment of joy breaking through the grim narrative. Structurally, 'sight' is the hinge on which the poem turns, marking the transition from waking suffering to visionary liberation. The word also implicitly contrasts with the speaker's daytime 'cry': where weeping is the sound of suffering, sight is the possibility of seeing beyond it. Blake frames the imagination as a form of seeing that transcends the material conditions of oppression — even if, as the poem's ending will suggest, such seeing may be manipulated.
ex_en: Tom fell asleep that night and experienced a powerful sight — a dream-vision so vivid it felt like a revelation.
ex_zh: The sight arrived without warning — / a crack in the ceiling that let through / not water but a shaft of blue / that tasted like forgiveness.

def_en: long, narrow boxes in which dead bodies are buried; here, a metaphor for the chimneys that entrap the child sweepers
def_zh: coffins of black, locked in coffins, opened the coffins
quote_en: "That thousands of sweepers, Dick, Joe, Ned, & Jack, / Were all of them locked up in coffins of black;" – In Tom's dream, he sees countless chimney-sweeps, named individually, imprisoned inside black coffins that represent the chimneys themselves (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'coffins' transforms the chimney from a site of labour into a site of death. The metaphor is horrifyingly literal: chimney-sweeps regularly died trapped in flues, suffocated by soot or crushed by collapsing masonry. The word 'coffins' makes explicit what the waking narrative only implies — that this work is fatal, that the chimneys swallow children. The colour adjective 'black' is both descriptive (soot-stained) and symbolic (the blackness of death, of evil, of a world without divine light). The naming of individual children — 'Dick, Joe, Ned, & Jack' — before the collective noun 'thousands' is a poignant gesture: each coffin contains a specific, nameable child, not an anonymous statistic. The passive construction 'were all of them locked up' emphasises the children's powerlessness; they did not enter the coffins but were placed there. In the dream-logic, the coffins are the condition from which the children need liberation — the Angel's key will open them, but in waking life, no key exists.
ex_en: In Tom's dream-vision, thousands of chimney-sweepers, all known by name, were trapped inside black coffins like the chimneys they worked in.
ex_zh: My grandfather's war stories arrived in coffins — / small boxes of silence he opened at dinner, / each one releasing a dust / that settled on our potatoes like salt.

def_en: not imprisoned or enslaved; able to act at will; liberated from confinement
def_zh: set them free, break free, free at last
quote_en: "And by came an Angel who had a bright key, / And he opened the coffins & set them all free;" – In Tom's dream, an Angel arrives with a shining key to unlock the coffins and liberate all the trapped children (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake places 'free' at the moment of maximum hope in the poem — the Angel's act of liberation is the dream's emotional core. The word is positioned at the end of the couplet's second line, a rhyming partner to 'key', as if freedom and the key that unlocks it are linguistically inseparable. 'Free' carries both physical meaning (released from the coffin-chimneys) and spiritual meaning (emancipated from the bondage of sin and suffering). The Angel's bright key is a classic Blakean image of divine intervention, light breaking into darkness. Yet the word is deeply ambiguous: freedom is granted by an external authority (the Angel), not seized by the children themselves. And it exists only within the dream. The waking reality that follows will offer no such liberation. Blake thus uses 'free' both to articulate the children's deepest longing and to expose the gap between visionary promise and material reality — a gap that the poem's final stanza will exploit with savage irony.
ex_en: An Angel came with a shining key, unlocked the black coffins, and set all the child sweepers free from their imprisonment.
ex_zh: Free was a word she kept in a cage / and fed through the bars each morning — / it grew plump on syllables, / but never once opened its wings.

def_en: to give out a bright light; to glow or gleam with reflected light; to be radiant
def_zh: shine in the sun, wash and shine, eyes shine
quote_en: "Then down a green plain, leaping, laughing they run, / And wash in a river and shine in the Sun." – The liberated children run through a green meadow and wash themselves clean in a river, their bodies becoming radiant in the sunlight (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'shine' is the visual climax of the dream-vision — the moment when soot-blackened children become radiant bodies in the sun. The verb transforms the children from objects of pity into sources of light. 'Shine' is what soot cannot do; it is the property of clean water, healthy skin, the sun itself. Blake places it at the end of a kinetic sequence — leaping, laughing, running, washing — and lets it land as the culmination of all this liberated motion. The alliteration of 'shine' with 'Sun' binds the children to the divine light that bathes them. In the symbolic economy of Songs of Innocence, shining is a sign of spiritual grace, of the divine image restored. Yet the sun that makes them shine is the same sun that, in the waking world, lights the chimneys they must climb. Blake's vision is not escapist: the dream offers a glimpse of what the children should be, a standard against which the waking world's failure can be measured. 'Shine' is what innocence looks like when it is not covered in soot.
ex_en: The children ran laughing down a green field, washed themselves in the river, and began to shine brilliantly in the sunlight.
ex_zh: The boy who swept our chimney learned to shine / in the only way he could — / by smiling with all his teeth / until the soot on his cheeks cracked like old varnish.

def_en: to move from a lower position to a higher one; to ascend, get up, or be resurrected
def_zh: rise upon clouds, rise up, rise and shine
quote_en: "Then naked & white, all their bags left behind, / They rise upon clouds, and sport in the wind." – The children, now naked and clean, abandon their work-bags and ascend into the clouds to play freely in the wind (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'rise' is a word of resurrection and transcendence. In Christian iconography, the risen body ascends to heaven, and Blake draws on this tradition to imagine the chimney-sweeps' liberation as a kind of ascension. The children leave their 'bags' — the tools of their trade, the weight of their labour — and rise into an element of pure freedom. 'Rise' also echoes the daily reality of chimney-sweeping: the children 'rose in the dark' each morning to work. The dream inverts this grim rising into a rising toward light and play. The nakedness of the children is important: they have shed not only their bags but the clothes that marked them as sweeps, as workers, as owned. To 'rise' is to escape the gravity of exploitation, to become weightless. Blake places 'rise' at the centre of a couplet that brims with movement — the clouds, the wind, the sport — creating a vision of childhood restored to its natural state of joyful motion before it was sold into labour.
ex_en: The children left their work-bags behind, became clean and naked, and began to rise up on the clouds to play in the wind.
ex_zh: We rose from the argument like smoke — / no longer solid enough to touch, / curling away from each other / into separate weather systems.

def_en: a feeling of great happiness, pleasure, or delight
def_zh: never want joy, full of joy, bring joy
quote_en: "And the Angel told Tom, if he'd be a good boy, / He'd have God for his father & never want joy." – The Angel in Tom's dream makes a conditional promise: if Tom behaves well, he will have God as his father and will never lack for joy (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'joy' is the Angel's promised reward, a word that belongs to the vocabulary of innocence. In Songs of Innocence, joy is the natural state of the child who is loved and protected. Here, however, 'joy' is deferred and conditional: it depends on being 'a good boy', which in context means accepting one's suffering without complaint. The Angel's promise is deeply troubling. It echoes the ideology used to pacify the exploited — suffer now, be rewarded later; obey, and joy will come. Blake, a radical critic of organised religion, places this promise in the mouth of an Angel not to endorse it but to expose how religious consolation can serve as a tool of social control. 'Never want joy' is a magnificent promise that masks a cruel bargain: the child must die to receive it. The dream's vision of liberation is undercut by this final condition, which reasserts the authority the dream seemed to overthrow. 'Joy' becomes not a right but a reward for obedience.
ex_en: The Angel told Tom that if he was a good boy, God would become his father and he would never lack joy for the rest of his life.
ex_zh: Joy was an old coin she found in her mother's coat / after the funeral — / it still bought something, / though all the shops had changed their names.

def_en: the absence of light; nightfall; a state of gloom, ignorance, or obscurity
def_zh: rose in the dark, before the dark, out of the dark
quote_en: "And so Tom awoke; and we rose in the dark / And got with our bags & our brushes to work." – Tom wakes from his dream and the children rise before dawn, gathering their tools to begin another day of labour (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake's 'dark' extinguishes the dream's radiance in a single word. Throughout the vision, the poem was filled with light — the bright key, the green plain, the shining sun, the white clouds. 'Dark' restores the material conditions of the sweeps' lives: they rise before dawn, in the absence of light, to begin their labour. The word also carries moral and spiritual resonance. In Blake's symbolic system, darkness is the state of a world that has lost the divine vision, a world where children are sold and chimneys become coffins. The verb 'rose' echoes the children's earlier rising into clouds, but now 'rose in the dark' is a deflating parody of that earlier ascension — a sinking rather than a soaring. Blake positions 'dark' at the caesura, a heavy pause after 'awoke', so the word lands with the full weight of waking disappointment. The dream is over. The dark remains.
ex_en: Tom woke up and the boys rose in the dark of early morning, gathered their brushes and bags, and went back to work.
ex_zh: The dark in our house was not the absence of light / but a presence — it sat in the armchair, / it folded the laundry, / it answered the phone before we could.

def_en: a moral or legal obligation; a task or action that one is required to perform
def_zh: do their duty, sense of duty, bound by duty
quote_en: "Though the morning was cold, Tom was happy & warm; / So if all do their duty, they need not fear harm." – The speaker concludes that Tom was comforted by his dream, and that those who perform their obligations faithfully have nothing to fear (The Chimney-Sweeper, 1789)
quote_zh: Blake closes with 'duty', the most bitterly ironic word in the poem. Placed in the mouth of the innocent child-narrator, it reproduces the moralising platitude that keeps the exploited compliant. 'Duty' means accepting one's lot, working without complaint, trusting in divine reward. The child speaker offers this as comfort, unaware that he is parroting the ideology of his oppressors. Blake's contemporary readers would have recognised the phrase 'do their duty' as the language of church, state, and charitable institutions — all of which preached obedience to the poor while benefiting from their labour. The couplet's logic is chilling: Tom is 'happy & warm' not because his circumstances have changed (the morning is 'cold') but because his dream has made him pliable. 'Duty' transforms exploitation into virtue, suffering into sacrifice. By ending the poem on this word, Blake exposes how innocence can be weaponised against itself — the sweeper who believes in duty will sweep until his lungs are destroyed, never fearing harm because he has been promised a heaven that never arrives.
ex_en: Despite the cold morning, Tom felt warm and happy because of his dream, and the speaker concludes that children who do their duty faithfully need not be afraid of harm.
ex_zh: Duty was the name my grandmother gave / to the locked drawer in her dressing table — / we never saw inside, / but we polished the key every Sunday.

def_en: particular locations, regions, or areas; the physical environments where people live or come from
def_zh: made of places, familiar places, sense of places
quote_en: "People are made of places. They carry with them / hints of jungles or mountains, a tropic grace" – The speaker opens with a declaration that human identity is formed by the landscapes people inhabit and carry within them (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster opens with 'places', a deceptively simple word that carries the poem's entire thesis. She chooses the plural, insisting that identity is not formed by a single location but by multiple environments that layer and accumulate. The word is deliberately general, encompassing both the 'jungles' and 'mountains' of the next line and the specific pine woods and farmhouses that will follow. 'Places' also establishes the poem's democratic impulse: everyone is made of somewhere; geography is a universal constituent of selfhood. The short declarative sentence — 'People are made of places' — has the force of a philosophical axiom. It echoes the language of elemental composition (people are made of flesh, of bone, of dust) and elevates place to the same fundamental status. Brewster uses 'places' to challenge the idea that identity is primarily social, psychological, or economic; before all of these, it is geographical. The word also prepares for the poem's central contrast between urban and rural places, suggesting that the difference between people is, at root, a difference of landscape.
ex_en: People are shaped by the places they come from and carry subtle reminders of those landscapes with them wherever they go.
ex_zh: The places we left behind grew restless / in our absence — they rearranged the furniture, / renamed the streets, / and pretended they had never known our footprints.

def_en: the pervading tone or mood of a place; the air or climate of a particular environment
def_zh: atmosphere of cities, heavy atmosphere, changed atmosphere
quote_en: "or the cool eyes of sea-gazers. Atmosphere of cities / how different drops from them," – The speaker contrasts the air that clings to city-dwellers with the natural grace carried by those from wilder places (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster selects 'atmosphere' to shift the poem from visible geography to the invisible, ambient quality that places imprint on people. The word is meteorological before it is psychological: it means the literal air, the gases surrounding a body. By applying it to cities, Brewster suggests that urban dwellers carry a distinctive climate — one that 'drops from them' like condensation or precipitation. The verb 'drops' is striking and faintly unpleasant, suggesting something that seeps or falls away from the body involuntarily. 'Atmosphere' also carries the literary meaning of mood or tonal quality, and the poem exploits both senses: the city has a physical atmosphere (smog, smell) and an emotional atmosphere (pressure, haste). The enjambment that isolates 'of cities' at the start of line 3 creates a brief pause, a hovering, before the qualifying 'how different' — as if the reader must first absorb the weight of the urban atmosphere before judging it.
ex_en: The atmosphere of city life clings to its inhabitants and falls away from them in a way that is utterly different from the air of natural places.
ex_zh: The atmosphere in that house learned to hold its breath / whenever the front door opened — / even the curtains froze mid-sway, / waiting to see which version of him walked in.

def_en: in a neat, orderly, and well-organised manner; with everything in its proper place
def_zh: tidily plotted, arranged tidily, tidily organised
quote_en: "or the almost-not-smell of tulips in the spring, / nature tidily plotted with a guidebook;" – The speaker describes urban nature as something controlled and catalogued, stripped of wildness and reduced to a tourist attraction (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster's 'tidily' is the poem's sharpest critical adverb, a word that exposes what is lost when nature is urbanised. Flowers in a city are planted in designated beds, mapped in guidebooks, appreciated in an orderly fashion. 'Tidily' suggests a nature that has been house-trained, made compliant, stripped of its power to overwhelm or terrify. The word carries a faintly domestic, even fussy quality — tidiness is a virtue of cupboards and sock drawers, not of forests and mountains. It contrasts violently with the 'burned-out bush' and 'frosty wind' that will characterise the speaker's rural landscape later in the poem. The adverb also modifies 'plotted', a verb that suggests both mapping and scheming, as if the tidiness of urban nature is a small conspiracy against wildness. Brewster does not condemn the tulips themselves — the 'almost-not-smell' is delicate, almost beautiful — but the frame that contains them, the guidebook logic that reduces nature to information.
ex_en: In the city, nature is tidily arranged and documented, like tulips in spring whose faint scent is catalogued in a guidebook for tourists.
ex_zh: She tidily folded her desires / into squares small enough to fit / inside the pockets of a life / that had stopped being hers years ago.

def_en: the faculty or sense of perceiving odours; a distinctive odour, especially one that triggers memory
def_zh: smell of work, smell of smog, smell of subways
quote_en: "or the smell of work, glue factories maybe, / chromium-plated offices; smell of subways / crowded at rush hours." – The speaker catalogues the industrial and bodily odours that constitute the sensory memory of urban life (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster makes 'smell' the dominant sensory mode of the city, and the choice is pointed. Smell is the most involuntary and intimate of the senses — you can close your eyes, but you cannot stop breathing. By cataloguing the smells of urban life, Brewster suggests that the city enters the body whether one wants it to or not. 'Smell of work' is a remarkable phrase, yoking together the sensory and the economic. Work, in the speaker's vision, is not just an activity but an odour — of glue, of metal, of bodies pressed together. The list moves from the specific ('glue factories') to the sleekly modern ('chromium-plated offices') to the collective ('subways crowded at rush hours'), each smell carrying a different class and type of labour. The repetition of 'smell' (used twice) creates an incantatory effect, as if the speaker is trying to capture something that language can only approximate. Smell is the sense most closely linked to memory, and by grounding urban identity in smell, Brewster insists that place lives in the body at a pre-verbal level.
ex_en: The city leaves its mark on people through the smell of factories, offices, and crowded subways at rush hour.
ex_zh: The smell of my grandfather's workshop / stayed in his coat for thirty years — / sawdust, tobacco, and something metallic / that tasted like patience.

def_en: to hold and transport from one place to another; to keep something present in one's mind or memory
def_zh: carry woods, carry with them, carry in their minds
quote_en: "Where I come from, people / carry woods in their minds, acres of pine woods;" – The speaker contrasts her rural homeland with the city, asserting that her people carry vast forests in their memory rather than urban atmospheres (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster returns to 'carry', echoing the word from line one ('They carry with them hints'), but now the object is magnified from 'hints' to 'woods' and 'acres'. This repetition with variation is the poem's structural hinge: the verb remains the same, but the scale and specificity of what is carried transforms. Rural people do not carry mere traces or atmospheres; they carry entire landscapes, measured in acres, present in their minds. 'Carry' is a verb of labour — one carries a burden, a weight — and Brewster honours the effort of memory. To carry woods in the mind is not effortless nostalgia but a form of work, a continual act of attention and preservation. The word also suggests portability and movement: the rural person who moves to the city does not lose the woods but transports them internally. 'Carry' thus becomes a form of resistance to displacement, a way of refusing to let go of the place that made you even when you are no longer in it.
ex_en: The people from the speaker's rural homeland carry entire landscapes inside their minds, holding acres of pine forest in their memory wherever they go.
ex_zh: She carried her mother's voice for decades — / not the words, which she had long forgotten, / but the frequency, the particular hum / that vibrated just below language.

def_en: without purpose, direction, or goal; in a wandering, unfocused manner
def_zh: clucking aimlessly, wandering aimlessly, drifting aimlessly
quote_en: "wooden farmhouses, old, in need of paint, / with yards where hens and chickens circle about, / clucking aimlessly;" – The speaker describes the unhurried, purposeless movement of farm animals in the rural yards of her memory (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster's 'aimlessly' is a quietly radical word in a poem that contrasts rural and urban life. In the city, everything has an aim — rush hours, plotted nature, the purposeful smell of work. But the hens in the farmyard circle without goal, 'clucking aimlessly', and this lack of purpose is not a failing but a quality to be cherished. The adverb refuses the logic of productivity that governs urban existence. 'Aimlessly' suggests a different way of being in the world, one not oriented toward output or efficiency. The hens do not need a reason to circle; their movement is its own justification. The word also carries a faint melancholy — aimlessness can be a symptom of loss, of not knowing where to go — but Brewster seems to offer it as a form of freedom. The alliteration of 'clucking aimlessly' with the hard 'c' of 'circle' and 'clucking' creates a small sonic loop, a circular sound that enacts the movement it describes.
ex_en: Around the old farmhouses, hens wandered without purpose, circling the yard and clucking aimlessly as they went about their day.
ex_zh: My thoughts on Sunday afternoons grew aimlessly / like ivy on an abandoned fence — / they did not know where the fence ended / and the sky began, and they did not care.

def_en: frozen water; a state of frozenness, stillness, or suspended life
def_zh: breaking of ice, ice and snow, fields of ice
quote_en: "Spring and winter / are the mind's chief seasons: ice and the breaking of ice." – The speaker declares that the rural mind is governed by two seasons and one elemental image: ice frozen solid and ice breaking apart (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster's 'ice' is the poem's most compressed and powerful image of the rural psyche. Where the urban catalogue was a sprawl of smells and surfaces, the rural mind is distilled to a single element. Ice is both substance and metaphor: it is the literal winter landscape of the speaker's homeland (New Brunswick, Canada) and a figure for emotional and psychological states — hardness, endurance, frozenness, the long wait for thaw. The phrase 'ice and the breaking of ice' is extraordinary in its compression. It names not just a season but a drama: the long period of frozen stasis and the violent, liberating moment when ice cracks. 'Breaking' is an active, almost violent verb — ice does not melt gently in this landscape; it breaks. The mind, Brewster suggests, is shaped by this rhythm of freeze and fracture. The monosyllable 'ice' itself is hard, sharp, a single syllable that sits in the mouth like a chip of frozen water. It contrasts with the multisyllabic, sprawling urban smells and establishes the rural mind as something starker, more elemental, less cluttered.
ex_en: In the rural mind, only two seasons matter — spring and winter — and the defining experience is the long ice and its eventual breaking.
ex_zh: Ice formed between them so slowly / neither noticed until they tried to speak / and their words came out cracked, / each syllable skating on its own frozen pond.

def_en: a hinged barrier used to open and close an entrance; a means of access or opportunity
def_zh: door blows open, open the door, door in the mind
quote_en: "A door in the mind blows open, and there blows / a frosty wind from fields of snow." – The speaker closes with an image of memory as a door that suddenly swings open, letting in a freezing wind from the landscape of the past (Where I Come From, 1982)
quote_zh: Brewster closes with 'door', an image that transforms the entire poem's logic. Throughout, people have 'carried' places, as if memory were a possession held within. But a door is not a container; it is a threshold, a boundary between inside and outside. The mind, in this final couplet, is not a storehouse of place-memories but a space susceptible to sudden, involuntary invasion. The repetition of 'blows' — the door blows open, the wind blows through — creates a terrifying symmetry: the mind opens, and the landscape enters with the force of weather. 'Door' suggests both vulnerability (the mind can be breached) and possibility (the door opens onto something vast). The adjective 'frosty' and the 'fields of snow' return the poem to the ice and cold of the rural landscape, but now that landscape has agency — it comes for the mind, whether invited or not. Brewster ends not with a nostalgic recollection but with a startling event, a door caught by a gust, and the past rushing in like winter air. The image is both beautiful and threatening, and it refuses to resolve the poem's urban-rural tension into easy consolation.
ex_en: A door inside the mind suddenly blows open and a frosty wind rushes through from the snowy fields of the remembered landscape.
ex_zh: There is a door in every childhood / that only opens from the other side — / and sometimes, in the small hours, / you hear the latch lift without knowing whose hand it is.

def_en: killed by depriving of air; suppressed or stifled completely
def_zh: smothered by smog, smothered cries, smothered flames
quote_en: "You should be here, Nature has need of you. / She has been laid waste. Smothered by the smog, / the flowers are mute, and the birds are few / in a sky slowing like a dying clock." – The speaker summons Wordsworth to witness an environmental emergency: Nature is being suffocated by pollution (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey opens with 'smothered', a verb of active, ongoing killing that sets the poem's tone of suffocation. The word derives from 'smother', meaning to deprive of air, and the assonance with 'smog' in the same line makes the agent of suffocation audibly present. 'Smothered' transforms pollution from an abstract problem into a physical assault — the flowers are not just damaged but are being choked to death. The passive construction ('Smothered by the smog') emphasises Nature's helplessness; there is an unnamed agent, the polluting force of human industry, that presses down and denies breath. Boey also invokes the Petrarchan tradition of mute flowers and silent nature, but radicalises it: these flowers are not metaphorically silent but literally dying. The word also prefigures the poem's later verbs — 'choked', 'stifled' — creating a lexicon of asphyxiation that culminates in the final 'death of poetry and of the earth'. 'Smothered' is the first breath the poem itself struggles to take.
ex_en: The speaker calls Wordsworth to witness how Nature has been smothered by thick smog, silencing the flowers and driving the birds away beneath a failing sky.
ex_zh: The city smothered its own morning — / wrapped the sunrise in grey gauze, / tucked it into a bed of exhaust fumes, / and told the birds to lower their voices.

def_en: placed in a tomb; buried; confined as if in a grave
def_zh: entombed in waste, lies entombed, entombed alive
quote_en: "All hopes of Proteus rising from the sea / have sunk; he is entombed in the waste / we dump." – The speaker declares that the shape-shifting sea-god Proteus has not merely vanished but is buried alive in the ocean's pollution (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey selects 'entombed' over simpler alternatives like 'buried' or 'trapped' to invoke the weight and finality of a grave. A tomb is a deliberate structure, a sealed chamber for the dead. By placing Proteus — the Greek sea-god who could change shape at will and who embodied the ocean's transformative power — inside a tomb of 'waste', Boey makes pollution an act of deicide, the killing of a god. The word carries echoes of Christ's entombment, a myth of death and possible resurrection, but the hopes have already 'sunk'; resurrection is not promised. The enjambment that separates 'waste' from 'we dump' places the agent — humanity — at the start of the next line, a delayed accusation. 'Entombed' also points to the poem's larger concern: classical myth and Romantic nature-worship are being buried by modernity's garbage. The gods that once animated the natural world are not merely absent; they are sealed in the very refuse humanity discards carelessly.
ex_en: The sea-god Proteus has not simply disappeared; he is now entombed in the waste that humans dump into the ocean.
ex_zh: My grandfather's letters are entombed in a shoebox / beneath a collapsing attic roof — / the ink is slowly turning to soil, / and the soil is learning to read.

def_en: obstructed in the throat or airway; prevented from breathing or sounding
def_zh: horns are choked, choked with waste, voice choked
quote_en: "Triton's notes struggle to be free, / his famous horns are choked, his eyes are dazed," – The speaker describes how the sea-god Triton's conch-shell trumpet is blocked and silenced by pollution (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey's 'choked' is the second verb of asphyxiation in the poem's catalogue, following 'smothered' and anticipating 'stifled'. The word is visceral and pharyngeal — it constricts the throat even as one reads it. Triton, the herald of the sea whose conch-shell horn calmed waves and announced the ocean's power, can no longer sound his instrument. The horns are 'choked' — a word that applies both to the physical blockage of the shell and to the strangulation of a living throat. Boey collapses the distinction between instrument and body: Triton is his horn, and the horn is choking. The alliteration of 'horns' and 'choked' is deliberately harsh, the aspirated 'h' cut off by the hard plosive 'k' sound, enacting phonetically the stoppage the word describes. 'Choked' also carries a submerged political resonance — to choke someone is an act of violence, and the poem implicitly accuses the polluters of throttling the natural world. In a poem addressed to Wordsworth, 'choked' is also an anti-Romantic word: where the Romantic wind played through Aeolian harps, here it cannot pass at all.
ex_en: Triton's famous conch-shell horns are choked and blocked with waste so that his music can no longer sound freely across the waters.
ex_zh: Her apology choked halfway up her throat / and stayed there for three years, / growing hard and smooth as a pearl / she could neither swallow nor spit out.

def_en: never satisfied; having an appetite or desire that cannot be quenched
def_zh: insatiate man, insatiate greed, insatiate hunger
quote_en: "and Neptune lies helpless as a beached whale, / while insatiate man moves in for the kill." – The speaker portrays humanity as a predator with an appetite that can never be satisfied, closing in on the defenceless god of the sea (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey chooses 'insatiate', an archaic and literary form of 'insatiable', to lend biblical gravity to his indictment. The older form echoes the language of the King James Bible and of Milton, elevating the accusation of greed into a timeless moral failing. 'Insatiate' means incapable of being satisfied, and Boey applies it not to a particular industry or corporation but to 'man' in the collective — all of humanity is implicated. The word diagnoses the engine of environmental destruction: not need, not survival, but a hunger that by definition can never be met. 'Insatiate' positioned directly before 'man' creates a heavy, damning rhythm, the five syllables of the adjective overwhelming the monosyllable of its subject. The word also connects to the poem's catalogue of wounded gods: Neptune is 'helpless', Triton is 'dazed', Proteus is 'entombed' — and the force that has done this to them is not accident but appetite. Humanity 'moves in for the kill' not from necessity but from a craving that has no end.
ex_en: Neptune lies helpless like a stranded whale while humanity, driven by an appetite that can never be satisfied, moves in to finish him off.
ex_zh: Insatiate, the city ate its own history — / digesting brick by brick, / spitting out glass towers, / and calling the indigestion progress.

def_en: (of an illness) persisting for a long time or constantly recurring; long-lasting and difficult to eradicate
def_zh: chronic wounds, chronic illness, chronic condition
quote_en: "Poetry and piety have begun to fail, / as Nature's heart grows weaker at the will / of human greed. The wounds are chronic." – The speaker declares that Nature's injuries are not temporary but deep, enduring, and possibly permanent (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey deploys 'chronic' with clinical precision, borrowing from medical vocabulary to diagnose the condition of the earth. The word distinguishes between acute and chronic illness: an acute wound is sudden and may heal; a chronic wound is ongoing, systemic, and resists cure. By calling the wounds 'chronic', Boey rejects the possibility of quick or easy remediation. The damage is not a single event that can be reversed but a condition embedded in the body of the world. The short declarative sentence — 'The wounds are chronic' — lands with the finality of a diagnosis delivered by a physician. The full stop after 'chronic' creates a heavy pause, as if the poem itself must stop to absorb the gravity of the prognosis. 'Chronic' also applies to the human condition that caused the wounds: greed is not a momentary lapse but a chronic feature of human behaviour. The word thus links the illness of the earth to the illness of the species that inhabits it. In a poem lamenting the failure of poetry and piety, 'chronic' suggests that this failure too is not temporary but structural.
ex_en: The wounds inflicted upon Nature by human greed are chronic, persisting and worsening as the heart of the natural world grows steadily weaker.
ex_zh: Her loneliness was chronic — / not the sharp kind that visits and leaves, / but the dull ache that moves in, / unpacks its bags, and learns to cook in your kitchen.

def_en: a remedy or treatment that restores health; a solution to a problem or affliction
def_zh: beyond cure, find a cure, cure for
quote_en: "The sick / rivers run to a sea of death, and the cure / is now beyond the Greats, for what can Poetry do" – The speaker admits that the possibility of healing now lies beyond the reach of even the greatest poets and thinkers of the past (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey places 'cure' at the end of a line break, suspended between the 'sick rivers' that precede it and the despairing admission that follows — 'is now beyond the Greats'. The enjambment enacts the poem's central anxiety: cure is dangled as a possibility only to be snatched away. The word is the hinge on which the poem's hope turns into despair. 'Cure' belongs to the vocabulary of medicine and healing, and its presence suggests that the poem has been a diagnosis all along. But the doctor — Poetry, the Greats, Wordsworth — is impotent. The 'Greats' are capitalised, invoking the canon of Western literature, and the declaration that even they cannot provide a cure is a radical admission of art's limits. Boey, a Singaporean poet writing in the shadow of the British Romantic tradition, confronts the possibility that poetry itself is inadequate to the scale of ecological catastrophe. 'Cure' thus becomes a word of loss — not just the loss of environmental health but the loss of faith in art's redemptive power.
ex_en: The sick rivers flow into a dying sea, and any possible cure for this devastation is now beyond the reach of even the greatest poets of the past.
ex_zh: The cure arrived too late and in the wrong language — / a prescription written in a script / the pharmacists had stopped learning / the year the old country became a province.

def_en: made unable to breathe; suffocated; suppressed or silenced by force
def_zh: stifled by mud, stifled cries, stifled growth
quote_en: "amid the dress code, the loud music, the thud / of heavy metal and wasteful industry? / The flowers are muted, stifled by the mud." – The speaker returns to the image of silenced flowers, now stifled into silence not by smog but by mud (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey's 'stifled' completes the poem's trilogy of asphyxiation verbs — 'smothered', 'choked', 'stifled' — each one tightening the grip on the poem's breath. Where 'smothered' described the flowers at the poem's opening, 'stifled' returns to them near its close, but with a crucial difference: the agent has shifted from smog to mud. Mud is earth mixed with water, the primordial substance of creation, now become an instrument of suffocation. The word 'stifled' carries the connotation of deliberate suppression, of a voice prevented from speaking. The flowers, earlier described as 'mute', are now actively prevented from sounding. This progression from muteness to stifling marks the worsening of the environmental crisis across the poem's span. 'Stifled' also applies to poetry itself: the question 'what can Poetry do' is followed by the image of stifled flowers, as if art itself is being silenced by the same forces that choke the natural world. The word sits in a list of modern distractions — 'dress code', 'loud music', 'heavy metal' — and suggests that amidst the noise of consumer culture, the voice of nature and of poetry is being actively extinguished.
ex_en: The flowers, surrounded by the noise of industry and waste, are stifled and silenced beneath a suffocating layer of mud.
ex_zh: Her talent was stifled so gently / she did not notice until she was forty — / a pillow pressed over a flame / by hands that claimed they were tucking her in.

def_en: made smaller, less, or weaker; reduced in size, importance, or intensity
def_zh: diminished earth, diminished world, diminished life
quote_en: "Wordsworth, look and tell us what you see / on this diminished earth, among the debris, / the dying soil and the polluted waters." – The speaker directly addresses Wordsworth and commands him to survey a planet that has shrunk in its vitality and grandeur (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey chooses 'diminished' with care; it is not 'destroyed' or 'ruined' but 'diminished' — a word that suggests erosion, reduction, a loss that may be gradual but is undeniable. The prefix 'di-' (a variant of 'dis-') signals separation and dispersal, as if the earth's fullness has been scattered and thinned. 'Diminished' also carries musical connotations: a diminished chord is one that has been reduced by a semitone, creating a sound of tension and melancholy. Applied to the earth, the word suggests that the planet is playing in a minor key, its harmonies disrupted. Boey places 'diminished' before 'earth' as an epithet, a permanent adjective that now modifies the planet in the way 'green' or 'blue' once did. The word also speaks to the poem's central conversation with Wordsworth, whose vision of nature was one of plenitude and sublime excess. To address Wordsworth and point to a 'diminished earth' is to stage a confrontation between Romantic abundance and contemporary scarcity. The earth has not ended; it has been lessened, and this lessening is perhaps more tragic than catastrophe because it passes unremarked.
ex_en: The speaker asks Wordsworth to look upon a diminished earth, reduced to debris, dying soil, and polluted waters, and to report what he witnesses.
ex_zh: The diminished man still wore his father's coat — / it hung from his shoulders like a country / that had lost half its provinces / but still remembered the shape of its old borders.

def_en: marked with a scar or scars; permanently damaged or disfigured
def_zh: scarred sky, heavily scarred, scarred landscape
quote_en: "The gods are scattered like migrating birds, / and the very sky is scarred. Nature, your ward, / is sick." – The speaker declares that the gods have fled and the sky itself now bears permanent marks of injury (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey's 'scarred' is a word of permanent and visible damage. A scar is what remains after a wound has healed — it is both a sign of survival and a record of injury. To say the sky is scarred is to claim that even the most infinite and intangible part of the natural world has been marked by human action. The word follows the image of gods 'scattered like migrating birds', a simile that transforms divine presence into a diaspora, a flight from a ruined homeland. 'Scarred' then takes this damage from the mythological to the visible: the sky itself — the very thing Wordsworth looked to for intimations of the sublime — now bears wounds. The alliteration of 'sky' and 'scarred' is harsh and binding, the sibilant 's' stretched across the caesura. 'Scarred' also connects to the earlier medical imagery of 'chronic' wounds: scars are the evidence that wounds were real, that they happened, that the body — even a celestial body — remembers. By calling Nature 'your ward', Boey reminds Wordsworth of his self-appointed role as Nature's protector, and the scarred sky is the evidence of that guardianship's failure.
ex_en: The gods have scattered like migrating birds leaving a scarred sky behind them, and Nature, once entrusted to your care, is gravely ill.
ex_zh: The scarred sky at sunset bled colours / no painter had ever named — / vermillion of old burns, / the purple of wounds that closed but never healed.
