def_en: the complete final destruction of the world; an event of great devastation and revelation
def_zh: coming century is Apocalypse, vision of Apocalypse, slow Apocalypse
quote_en: "The flowers, / the food of the spirit, smell of poison. / The coming century is Apocalypse, / the death of poetry and of the earth." – The speaker closes with a prophecy: the new century will bring not renewal but revelation of total destruction (Report to Wordsworth, 1997)
quote_zh: Boey closes with 'Apocalypse', a word that is both a terminus and a revelation. In its Greek root, 'apokalypsis' means an unveiling, a disclosure of what was hidden. The coming century, the speaker prophesies, will not bring progress but an uncovering of the destruction already underway. 'Apocalypse' is capitalised, lending it the weight of a proper name, a historical era — The Apocalypse — as if it is already a chapter title in a book not yet written. The word gathers all the poem's imagery of death and suffocation into a single eschatological frame. Flowers smelling of poison, the death of poetry and earth — these are not separate crises but manifestations of one comprehensive ending. By placing 'Apocalypse' in apposition with 'the death of poetry and of the earth', Boey equates the two: when the earth dies, poetry dies with it, because poetry is made of the earth's breath and body. The word also functions as a response to Wordsworth's faith in nature's renewing power — Boey offers not renewal but terminal diagnosis. Written in the late 1990s, the poem's 'coming century' is now our present, and the word 'Apocalypse' has lost none of its urgency.
ex_en: The new century will be an Apocalypse, bringing not hope but the final death of both poetry and the natural world.
ex_zh: Apocalypse arrived not with trumpets / but with a change in the weather report — / small print at the bottom of the screen / that no one stopped to read until the screen went black.

def_en: a heavy load; a duty or misfortune that causes hardship, worry, or grief
def_zh: pulsing burden, carry a burden, heavy burden
quote_en: "For the green turtle with her pulsing burden, / in search of the breeding-ground. / For her eggs laid in their nest of sickness." – The speaker laments the turtle swimming with her load of eggs through polluted waters toward a contaminated nesting place (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke opens with 'burden', a word that transforms the turtle's pregnancy from a sign of life into a weight of sorrow. The turtle's eggs — normally a symbol of renewal and continuity — are here a 'pulsing burden', the present participle 'pulsing' suggesting both the heartbeat of life and the throbbing of a wound. 'Burden' carries the connotation of something borne under duress, a load that exhausts the bearer. In the context of the Gulf War oil spills that inspired the poem, the turtle's burden is made tragic by its destination: the 'breeding-ground' has become a 'nest of sickness'. The word also functions as the poem's first implicit accusation — the burden is not natural but inflicted, the weight of human violence loaded onto a creature that cannot understand it. Clarke's choice of 'burden' over 'eggs' or 'young' sets the elegiac tone: this is not a celebration of new life but a lament for life already doomed. The word also echoes the refrain of the spiritual 'Balm in Gilead', connecting ecological grief to a longer tradition of lamentation.
ex_en: The green turtle carries her pulsing burden of eggs through polluted waters toward a breeding-ground that has become a nest of sickness.
ex_zh: She carried her childhood like a burden / that grew heavier each year — / not because the memories were heavy / but because she had forgotten how to put them down.

def_en: a ceremony or service held to honour and bury a dead person; here, a metaphor for the cormorant's oil-stained plumage
def_zh: funeral silk, funeral procession, funeral rites
quote_en: "For the cormorant in his funeral silk, / the veil of iridescence on the sand, / the shadow on the sea." – The speaker mourns the cormorant whose oil-drenched feathers resemble the black silk garments of mourning (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke's 'funeral' is the poem's governing metaphor made explicit. The cormorant's natural iridescence — the oily sheen on healthy feathers — is transformed by actual oil into 'funeral silk', the black crepe worn by Victorian mourners. The image is devastating because it collapses the distance between the living bird and its own death: the cormorant is already dressed for its funeral while still alive. 'Funeral' also establishes the poem's ritual quality. A lament is a funeral song, and by naming 'funeral silk' in the second stanza, Clarke signals that the entire poem will be a burial rite for the natural world. The word is followed by 'veil' and 'shadow', images of covering and darkness that deepen the funereal atmosphere. The sibilance of 'funeral silk' and 'iridescence' creates a whispering, hushed sound, as if the poem itself is speaking in the lowered tones of a mourner at a graveside. Clarke, writing in response to the 1991 Gulf War and its environmental devastation, uses 'funeral' to insist that what is being lost is not abstract 'biodiversity' but individual, beautiful, nameable creatures deserving of grief.
ex_en: The cormorant is already clothed in funeral garments, his oil-covered feathers like black silk mourning dress against the sand and sea.
ex_zh: The funeral was held in the old language — / the one the children no longer spoke — / so only the trees understood the eulogy, / and they wept sap for days afterwards.

def_en: causing or liable to cause death; fatal; (of a living thing) subject to death
def_zh: mortal stain, mortal wound, mortal danger
quote_en: "For the ocean's lap with its mortal stain. / For the heron grown stone." – The speaker laments the polluted ocean, whose gentle waves now carry a stain that brings death (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke chooses 'mortal' with devastating precision. The word means both 'causing death' and 'subject to death', and both meanings operate simultaneously. The oil stain on the ocean is mortal in the active sense — it kills, it brings death to everything it touches — and in the passive sense it marks the ocean as something that can now die, that has been stripped of its mythological immortality. 'Mortal stain' is an oxymoron that fuses the eternal (the ocean) with the terminal (death). 'Stain' suggests something that has penetrated below the surface, an indelible mark that cannot be washed clean. The word also carries a submerged religious resonance: mortal sin, the stain on the soul that leads to damnation. Clarke implicitly frames the oil spill as a sin against creation, not merely an environmental accident but a moral catastrophe. 'Mortal' sits at the centre of a line dedicated to the 'lap' of the ocean, a word of maternal gentleness now corrupted by death — the mother-body of the sea has been poisoned, and what laps at the shore now kills.
ex_en: The ocean's gentle waves now carry a mortal stain that brings death to everything they touch.
ex_zh: The mortal silence in the hospital room / was not the silence of sleep / but the silence of a clock / that had forgotten how to tick.

def_en: a comic performer in a circus, typically wearing a painted face and ridiculous costume; here, the puffin with its comical beak
def_zh: clown's face, painted clown, circus clown
quote_en: "For the puffin with his clown's face and painted beak." – The speaker singles out the puffin, whose comical, brightly coloured face resembles a circus performer in makeup (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: 'Clown' is the only extended simile in the poem's long litany of named creatures, and its placement is strategic. After nine bare names, Clarke suddenly pauses on the puffin to offer an image — a 'clown's face and painted beak' — and the effect is both tender and terrible. The word 'clown' captures the puffin's visual absurdity, its orange and black beak that looks applied with greasepaint. But a clown is also a figure of pathos, a performer whose comedy masks sadness. By calling the puffin a clown, Clarke suggests that the bird is performing for an audience that is destroying it, its bright face a mask that cannot protect it from oil and death. The word also carries the faintly pejorative sense of something not to be taken seriously — and the poem insists, by its very form, that the puffin must be taken seriously, must be mourned. In a list that includes majestic whales and sleek seals, 'clown' gives the puffin a different kind of dignity, the dignity of the ridiculous, the beloved, the irreplaceably strange.
ex_en: The puffin, with its ridiculous painted face and bright beak, resembled a circus clown performing unwittingly at the edge of a dying sea.
ex_zh: The clown in him surfaced at funerals — / cracking jokes while the coffin descended, / his painted smile the only thing / between the widow and the abyss.

def_en: a small, semi-aquatic rodent native to Britain, with glossy brown fur and a furry tail; Ratty from The Wind in the Willows
def_zh: water-vole habitat, endangered water-vole, water-vole burrow
quote_en: "For the whale. / For the dolphin. / For the seal. / For the otter. / For the water-vole." – The speaker's lament descends from the great marine mammals to the smallest, most vulnerable creatures of the waterways (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke includes 'water-vole' in the litany as a radical act of ecological democracy. The whale and dolphin command attention and sympathy; they are the charismatic megafauna of conservation campaigns. The water-vole — small, brown, unglamorous — is easily overlooked. By placing it alongside the whale and seal, Clarke insists that the lament does not rank its objects. The water-vole matters as much as the whale. The hyphenated form of the name is itself a small poem: 'water' and 'vole' joined by a tiny bridge, a creature defined by its relationship to its habitat. For British readers, the water-vole carries the ghost of Ratty from Kenneth Grahame's The Wind in the Willows, a beloved figure of childhood literature. To lament the water-vole is thus to lament not only a species but a whole imaginative world — the riverbank, the picnic basket, the 'messing about in boats' — now threatened by pollution and habitat loss. Clarke uses 'water-vole' to make grief specific and intimate: this is not an abstraction but a particular, nameable creature whose disappearance diminishes the world.
ex_en: Even the smallest creatures, like the water-vole, are included in this lament alongside the great whales and dolphins, for no loss is too small to mourn.
ex_zh: The last water-vole in the county / built her burrow from discarded receipts — / the paper tasted of ink and transactions, / but it held the river back for one more season.

def_en: a large, hairy social bee that flies with a loud hum, important for pollination
def_zh: bumble-bee buzz, bumble-bee nest, queen bumble-bee
quote_en: "For the dragonfly. / For the wren. / For the robin. / For the hare. / For the badger. / For the bumble-bee. / For the butterfly. / For the moth." – The speaker continues the lament, naming creatures of the air and land, from birds to insects (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke places 'bumble-bee' at the centre of a sequence that moves from birds to insects, and the word itself is a small sonic marvel. The doubled 'b' sounds — 'bumble-bee' — mimic the creature's low, vibrating hum, making the word an onomatopoeia of the life it names. To say 'bumble-bee' aloud is to feel the buzz in the lips and throat. Clarke chooses it, perhaps, for this very reason: the poem's lament is not only for the creatures but for the sounds they make, the sensory texture of a living world. A silent spring has no bumble-bee hum. The hyphenated form, like 'water-vole', joins two words into one creature, a linguistic act of creation that mirrors the biological act of pollination: the bumble-bee binds flower to flower, and the hyphen binds 'bumble' to 'bee'. The word also carries a faint absurdity — 'bumble' suggests clumsiness, a creature too heavy for its wings, which aerodynamics supposedly cannot explain. That this improbable, buzzing, furry thing should exist at all is a wonder; that it should be threatened is a particular kind of grief.
ex_en: Even the humble bumble-bee, with its heavy body and loud buzz, is named and mourned among the losses of the living world.
ex_zh: The last bumble-bee in the garden / flew in crooked spirals like a thumbed prayer wheel, / each rotation a syllable / of a language the flowers were forgetting how to speak.

def_en: a legless lizard native to Eurasia, often mistaken for a snake, with smooth, shiny scales
def_zh: slow worm basking, slow worm habitat, protected slow worm
quote_en: "For the ladybird. / For the spider. / For the harvest mouse. / For the slow worm. / For the adder. / For the mackerel. / For the carp. / For the eel. / For the salmon. / For the herring. / For the pilchard. / For the bass. / For the perch." – The speaker names a long sequence of creatures, including the obscure slow worm, a legless lizard many people have never seen (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke's 'slow worm' is perhaps the most unexpected name in the entire litany — a creature that is neither slow nor a worm but a legless lizard, cryptic and rarely seen, known mainly to naturalists and gardeners. Its inclusion is a radical gesture: the lament extends even to the unknown, the overlooked, the creature you might step past without noticing. 'Slow worm' is also a misnomer, a name that hides the creature's true nature, and in this it stands for all the creatures in the poem that humanity has misunderstood or failed to see. The word is plain, almost drab, two monosyllables joined without a hyphen, and this plainness is its power. Clarke refuses the temptation to name only the beautiful or the charismatic; she names the slow worm because it exists, because its existence is a fact of the world that deserves to be honoured and mourned. The slow worm, like the water-vole, also carries a specific Englishness — it is a creature of hedgerows and compost heaps, of the quiet, unnoticed margins of the countryside. To lose it is to lose a small piece of home.
ex_en: Even the slow worm, that legless lizard so often unseen and unremarked, is included in this lament for every living thing the earth is losing.
ex_zh: The slow worm in the compost heap / taught me that not everything that crawls / wants to be seen — some lives / are lived entirely in the warmth below the apple peel.

def_en: a permanent mark or design made on the skin with ink; here, the indelible stain of oil on feathers; also, a military drum signal
def_zh: sad tattoo, drum tattoo, tattoo of wind
quote_en: "For the tree of cinders. / For the starlings gone. / For the sad tattoo of the oily wind / on blackened, ruined breast feathers." – The speaker describes the sound and mark of the oil-laden wind against the bodies of dead birds (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke's 'tattoo' is the poem's most complex and surprising word, operating on multiple levels simultaneously. Primarily, it names the way oil stains feathers with the permanence of ink on skin — a marking that cannot be washed off, a defacement that will last as long as the body lasts. But 'tattoo' also carries the meaning of a military drumbeat, a rhythmic signal, and the 'sad tattoo of the oily wind' becomes a funeral march, the drum that accompanies the dead. The word thus fuses image and sound, the visual stain and the audible rhythm of destruction. 'Tattoo' also suggests the cultural practice of marking the body with ink, often as a sign of belonging or memory. Here, the oil imposes an unwelcome tattoo on the birds' feathers, marking them not with identity but with annihilation. The adjective 'sad' is deliberately plain, almost inadequate — and its inadequacy is the point, the understatement of grief that cannot find adequate language. Clarke places 'tattoo' in a stanza that also gives us 'tree of cinders' and 'starlings gone', building a landscape of aftermath where the wind itself has become an instrument of memorial.
ex_en: The oily wind beats a sad tattoo against the ruined feathers of the dead birds, marking them permanently like ink driven into skin.
ex_zh: The war left its tattoo on him — / not on his skin but on his sleep, / where every night the drumming started / at precisely the hour he had learned not to scream.

def_en: made silent; prevented from speaking, singing, or making sound
def_zh: silenced whales, silenced voices, forever silenced
quote_en: "For the burnt earth. / For the scorched ocean-bed. / For the blown birds. / For the silenced whales. / For the blinded fish. / For the vanished seals." – The speaker lists the participles of destruction, culminating in the whales whose song has been forcibly stopped (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke's 'silenced' is the most violent and personal verb in the catalogue of participles. 'Burnt' and 'scorched' and 'blown' and 'blinded' and 'vanished' describe what has been done to bodies and habitats, but 'silenced' describes what has been done to a voice. Whales sing — their songs travel hundreds of miles through the ocean, complex and evolving, a form of culture that humans are only beginning to understand. To silence a whale is not just to kill it but to stop its music, to end a tradition, to close a mouth that has been singing for millions of years. The word also carries political connotations: regimes silence dissidents; the powerful silence the vulnerable. Clarke implicitly frames the whales as political subjects, their silencing an act of oppression. The word's position in the list — after 'blown birds' and before 'blinded fish' — is a crescendo of sensory deprivation: the birds are destroyed in body, the whales are destroyed in voice, the fish are destroyed in sight. Each sense is taken in turn, and 'silenced' names the loss of sound, the quieting of the ocean's music into a vast and permanent stillness.
ex_en: The whales have been silenced, their great songs cut off, their voices that once filled the oceans now extinguished by human violence.
ex_zh: The piano sat silenced in the corner / for forty years after she left — / its keys gathering a dust / that was really music in retirement.

def_en: a dark area produced by a body blocking light; an insubstantial remnant; a vestige
def_zh: shadow on the sea, cast a shadow, shadow falls
quote_en: "For the earth soaked blind. / For the ocean's broken lap with its mortal stain. / For the cormorant in his funeral silk. / For the shadow on the sea." – The poem closes with a return to the opening images, ending on a single dark shape that lingers on the surface of the water (Lament, 1991)
quote_zh: Clarke closes 'Lament' with 'shadow', a word of pure absence. A shadow is not a thing but the interruption of light, the negative shape cast by a body standing between the sun and the earth. To end the poem on 'shadow' is to end not on a creature or even a death but on an emptiness, a darkness that testifies to something that was once there and is there no longer. The 'shadow on the sea' echoes the opening movement of the poem — 'the shadow on the sea' first appeared in the cormorant stanza — but now it stands alone, unmoored from any particular bird. It has become the shadow of everything that has been lost. The word also carries the connotation of a ghost, an after-presence, the trace of the vanished. Clarke's lament, which named creature after creature in an attempt to hold them in language, ends by acknowledging that language too can only cast a shadow — an echo, a memory, an outline of what has been. The final line, severed from syntax by a full stop, floats on the page like that shadow on the water, a dark mark that is both an ending and a memorial.
ex_en: All that remains is a shadow lying on the surface of the sea, the final ghost of everything that has been destroyed and lost.
ex_zh: His shadow stayed behind when he left — / it sits in the garden now, / a dark patch where nothing grows, / waiting for the sun to forgive it.

def_en: to walk with regular, measured steps, especially back and forth; to move restlessly
def_zh: start to pace, pace the floor, pace nervously
quote_en: "I watched a giant cockroach start to pace, / Skirting a ball of dust that rode the floor. / At first he seemed quite satisfied to trace / A path between the wainscot and the door," – The speaker observes a large cockroach walking back and forth with the restless, measured movement of an anxious human (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan opens with 'pace', a verb that immediately humanises the insect. Cockroaches scuttle, dart, or skitter — but this one paces, a word associated with worried people in waiting rooms, with caged animals, with the restless back-and-forth of anxious thought. By choosing 'pace', Halligan establishes the poem's governing conceit: the cockroach is not merely an insect but a mirror, its movements a physical manifestation of inner life. The word also sets up the poem's rhythm — the steady iambic pentameter that will carry the cockroach across the floor and through the stanzas. 'Pace' contains within it the idea of measurement (a pace is a unit of distance) and the idea of ritual (pacing out a boundary), and both senses are present: the cockroach traces a path, marks a territory, performs a small, compulsive ceremony of its own confinement. The 'ball of dust' it skirts is a perfect, miniature obstacle, and the cockroach's negotiation of it becomes a kind of purpose, 'quite satisfied' — until it is not.
ex_en: The giant cockroach began to pace the floor, moving restlessly back and forth while skirting around a drifting ball of dust.
ex_zh: He paced the length of his own argument / for forty years, wearing a groove / in the kitchen linoleum, / until the floor gave way to the cellar of everything unsaid.

def_en: bent, twisted, or out of shape; not straight; morally corrupt or dishonest
def_zh: crooked rings, crooked path, crooked smile
quote_en: "But soon he turned to jog in crooked rings, / Circling the rusty table leg and back, / And flipping right over to scratch his wings—" – The cockroach abandons his straight path for irregular, distorted circles around a table leg (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan's 'crooked' marks a turning point in the cockroach's behaviour and in the poem's psychology. Where the insect previously traced a straight, purposeful-seeming path, it now moves in 'crooked rings' — circles that are bent, irregular, disturbed. The word carries both physical meaning (not straight, misshapen) and moral meaning (dishonest, corrupt). Halligan activates both: the cockroach's path is crooked in shape, and the cockroach itself — in the human imagination — is a crooked creature, a skulking thing of filth and dishonesty. The 'crooked rings' also suggest a spiral, a movement that goes round and round without progressing, and this image will deepen as the poem unfolds into existential crisis. The hard plosive 'k' sound of 'crooked' echoes the clicking of insect legs on a hard floor, and the word sits uneasily in the line, its two syllables disrupting the iambic flow — a crooked word for a crooked movement. The 'rusty table leg' adds to the atmosphere of domestic decay, the ordinary furniture of a human room made strange by the cockroach's ritualised distress.
ex_en: The cockroach abandoned its straight path and began to jog in crooked rings, circling the rusty table leg in jagged, irregular loops.
ex_zh: Her crooked grief refused to run in straight lines — / it zigzagged through the house, / bumping into furniture, / knocking over photographs it had no right to touch.

def_en: the state of being unable to rest or relax; a persistent desire for change or movement
def_zh: attack of restlessness, inner restlessness, growing restlessness
quote_en: "As if a victim of a mild attack / Of restlessness that worsened over time. / After a while, he climbed an open shelf" – The speaker diagnoses the cockroach's erratic behaviour as a condition that begins mildly but intensifies (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan names 'restlessness' as the cockroach's affliction, and the word is the hinge on which the poem turns from observation to identification. 'Restlessness' is a profoundly human condition — the inability to be still, the itch for something one cannot name. By diagnosing the cockroach with restlessness, Halligan begins the process of collapsing the distance between watcher and watched. The word is carefully qualified: this is a 'mild attack' at first, something that could be dismissed or ignored, but it 'worsened over time'. The temporal dimension is crucial — this is not a sudden crisis but a slow, creeping condition, a chronic restlessness that has built to a breaking point. The noun form, abstract and clinical, contrasts with the vivid physicality of the preceding lines ('flipping right over', 'crooked rings'), as if the speaker is stepping back to offer a medical opinion on the insect's behaviour. 'Restlessness' also prepares the ground for the later vocabulary of discontent — 'unrest', 'irritable', 'discontent' — a lexicon of inner disturbance that will culminate in the poem's final, unanswerable question about cure.
ex_en: The cockroach seemed to be suffering from an attack of restlessness, a mild agitation that grew steadily worse as time passed.
ex_zh: Restlessness settled in her like a tenant / who paid no rent but rearranged the furniture, / played loud music at 3 a.m., / and refused to say when the lease would end.

def_en: not able to be relied on; not known or definite; lacking confidence or sureness
def_zh: looked uncertain, uncertain where, felt uncertain
quote_en: "And stopped. He looked uncertain where to climb. / Was this due payment for some vicious crime / A former life had led to?" – The cockroach pauses on a shelf, frozen by indecision, and the speaker wonders if this hesitation is karmic punishment (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan's 'uncertain' is the moment when animal instinct falters and something like consciousness flickers into view. The cockroach, which has been moving with increasing agitation, now stops entirely — and in that stopping, it 'looked uncertain'. The verb 'looked' is deliberately ambiguous: did the cockroach appear uncertain to the observer, or did it actively look with uncertainty in its gaze? Halligan leaves the question open, and the ambiguity is the point. The cockroach's uncertainty mirrors the speaker's own — the hesitation about direction, purpose, meaning. The word also introduces the poem's unexpected turn toward reincarnation and karma. The question 'Was this due payment for some vicious crime / A former life had led to?' reframes the cockroach's restlessness as something cosmic — a punishment, a working-out of moral debt. 'Uncertain' thus becomes not just a description of posture but a metaphysical condition. The insect does not know why it is restless, just as the human does not know why he is anxious. The pause in the line after 'stopped' enacts the hesitation: the verse itself becomes uncertain where to go next.
ex_en: The cockroach stopped climbing and looked uncertain about its next move, as if paralysed by a question it could not answer.
ex_zh: Uncertain of everything except the weight of her own hands, / she stood at the edge of the decision / like a swimmer who has forgotten / whether water is for drowning or for holding.

def_en: to move hurriedly with short, quick steps; to flee in haste
def_zh: quick to scurry, scurry away, scurry from
quote_en: "I, myself, / Had led a life as cockroach, quick to scurry / From burrow to folio, from cover to cover, / With nervous scratching at the truth," – The speaker reveals that he too has lived like a cockroach, hurrying from one hiding place to another while scratching anxiously at reality (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan's 'scurry' is the word that collapses the distance between man and insect. Where the cockroach 'paced' and 'jogged', the speaker describes his own movement as scurrying — a verb that belongs to vermin, to small creatures fleeing exposure. 'Scurry' carries connotations of fear, haste, and furtiveness. It is what one does when one does not want to be seen. The speaker has lived 'quick to scurry', always in motion, always evading. The preposition 'from burrow to folio' is a miniature autobiography: 'burrow' is an animal's hiding place, but 'folio' is a book, a manuscript, a page. The speaker is a writer, a scholar perhaps, and his scurrying has been from one text to another, from one 'cover to cover' — a life spent in libraries and archives, but described in the language of a hunted animal. The word 'scurry' degrades the intellectual life even as it describes it. The 'nervous scratching at the truth' that follows is both the cockroach's physical scratching and the writer's tentative, anxious approach to meaning. To scurry is to move without arriving, to stay always in the margins, never to stand still in the light.
ex_en: The speaker confesses that he too has lived like a cockroach, always ready to scurry from one hiding place to another, scratching nervously at the surface of truth.
ex_zh: My thoughts would scurry for cover / every time a big question entered the room — / they hid under the furniture of distraction / and refused to come out until the silence was safe again.

def_en: courage in the face of pain, adversity, or difficulty; endurance and strength of mind
def_zh: share fortitude, show fortitude, quiet fortitude
quote_en: "his hurry / To dodge the light, his fortitude, moreover, / He seemed to share;" – The speaker recognises in the cockroach not only its evasive haste but also a quality of endurance and courage (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan's 'fortitude' is an extraordinary word to apply to a cockroach, and the surprise is the point. 'Fortitude' belongs to the vocabulary of moral philosophy and heroic biography — it is one of the cardinal virtues, the courage that endures suffering without complaint. By claiming that the cockroach shares 'fortitude' with the human observer, Halligan radically elevates the insect. The cockroach is not merely a pest but a stoic, a creature that persists despite universal hostility and disgust. The word also carries a submerged irony: the cockroach's fortitude is precisely what makes it so hard to exterminate. It endures the poison, the shoe, the rolled-up newspaper, and it survives. This is a form of heroism, even if it is a heroism humanity despises. 'Fortitude' is placed mid-line, set off by commas, as if the speaker is pausing to consider the word before committing to it. The syntax of the surrounding lines — 'his hurry / To dodge the light, his fortitude, moreover, / He seemed to share' — is hesitant, qualified, as if the admission of kinship is being made reluctantly. But the word, once spoken, cannot be taken back. The cockroach has courage, and the speaker shares it, and the poem has become a confession of insect solidarity.
ex_en: The speaker recognised in the cockroach a quality of fortitude, a stubborn courage in the face of adversity that he himself seemed to possess.
ex_zh: Fortitude was not a roar but a whisper — / the sound of my grandmother's teaspoon / stirring tea she did not want / on the morning after the telegram came.

def_en: a state of dissatisfaction, disturbance, and agitation; a lack of peace or calm
def_zh: vague unrest, inner unrest, growing unrest
quote_en: "but at some front porch of his / A vague unrest had burrowed deep inside, / And he was wandering, looking for a sign— / Exhausted soon, and fighting to excrete / The irritation from his system." – The speaker imagines that somewhere in the cockroach's being, a formless discontent has taken root and drives its searching (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan deepens the diagnosis from 'restlessness' to 'unrest', a word with darker and more political connotations. 'Unrest' suggests civil disturbance, the stirring of rebellion, a state of affairs in which the established order is threatened. By locating 'a vague unrest' at 'some front porch of his', Halligan gives the cockroach an inner architecture — a house with a threshold, a domestic space that has been invaded by disorder. 'Vague' is crucial: the unrest has no clear object, no specific grievance, and this vagueness is what makes it so tormenting. The alliteration of 'vague' and 'burrowed' and 'deep inside' creates a subterranean music, the sound of something tunnelling below the surface. 'Unrest' also anticipates the poem's catalogue of filed emotions — 'Bored', 'Restless', 'Irritable', 'Discontent' — each one a more precise articulation of this initial, formless agitation. The cockroach is 'looking for a sign', and the religious echo is deliberate: it seeks revelation, meaning, a finger pointing the way, but finds only its own exhaustion.
ex_en: A vague unrest had burrowed deep into the cockroach's being, driving it to wander in search of some sign or relief it could not name.
ex_zh: The unrest in our house was not loud — / it was the quiet rearrangement of cutlery, / the drawer of sharp knives slid open / each night to check that nothing had been forgiven.

def_en: lack of contentment; dissatisfaction with one's circumstances; a restless longing for something better
def_zh: filed discontent, winter of discontent, simmering discontent
quote_en: "Over time / He'd filed the insect equivalent of "Bored," / "Restless," "Irritable," "Discontent," / And though he thought he knew full well what for, / He couldn't quite admit he didn't know the cure." – The speaker imagines the cockroach cataloguing its emotional states in an internal filing system, arriving at the most articulate diagnosis: discontent (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan's 'discontent' is the final item in the cockroach's emotional catalogue, and the most complex. The word is capitalised like the others — 'Bored', 'Restless', 'Irritable', 'Discontent' — as if it is a heading in a file, a bureaucratic label for a spiritual condition. 'Discontent' is the most articulate and adult of the four terms. Boredom is trivial; restlessness is physical; irritability is reactive. But discontent is existential — a settled, rational dissatisfaction with the conditions of one's existence. The word echoes the famous opening of Shakespeare's Richard III — 'Now is the winter of our discontent' — and carries with it the weight of that allusion: a state of profound and justified unhappiness with the world as it is. The irony is that the cockroach has 'filed' these emotions, as if understanding them intellectually could bring relief, but 'couldn't quite admit he didn't know the cure'. Discontent, by its nature, knows what is wrong but not how to fix it. The word captures the poem's central tragedy: the cockroach — and the human speaker — can name their condition with increasing precision, but naming does not heal.
ex_en: The cockroach had filed a record of its growing discontent alongside boredom, restlessness, and irritability, but could not admit it had no idea how to find a cure.
ex_zh: Discontent arrived in a grey suit, / sat down in the best chair, / and explained in calm, bureaucratic tones / exactly why the revolution had been postponed.

def_en: an attempt to make something that is not the case appear true; a false display or affectation
def_zh: loathing the pretence, keep up the pretence, strip away pretence
quote_en: "Looked up and facing me, he seemed to sense / Some buried presence in the room, a strange / Oppressive air, and loathing the pretence, / Scuttled off in haste towards the grating of a drain." – The cockroach looks directly at the speaker, senses something oppressive and false in the atmosphere, and flees in disgust (The Cockroach, 1998)
quote_zh: Halligan closes with 'pretence', a word that turns the poem's gaze back on the human observer. Throughout the poem, the speaker has been projecting onto the cockroach — diagnosing its restlessness, imagining its emotions, reading it as a mirror. But in the final stanza, the cockroach looks back, and what it senses is 'pretence'. The word is devastating because it suggests that the entire act of watching, the whole poetic exercise in sympathy and identification, has been a performance. The cockroach 'loathes' the pretence — the verb is strong, visceral, a word of active disgust — and flees. 'Pretence' also carries the suggestion of false claims to superiority. The human who watches the cockroach imagines himself as a detached observer, a scientific or philosophical eye, but the cockroach sees through the pose. The 'buried presence in the room' is perhaps the human's own unacknowledged self, the 'oppressive air' the weight of his own anxiety filling the space. The poem that began with the cockroach's restlessness ends with the cockroach's departure, and the word 'pretence' ensures that the reader understands who has been judged and found wanting. The 'grating of a drain' is the cockroach's exit, a return to the underworld from which it came, leaving the speaker alone with his stripped-away illusions.
ex_en: The cockroach looked up at the speaker, sensed the falseness and pretence in the room, and scuttled off in disgust toward the drain.
ex_zh: The pretence finally cracked on a Tuesday — / not with a bang but a small sigh / from the back of the throat, / where truth had been hiding all along.

def_en: formed into a globe or sphere; made round and full like a planet or world
def_zh: shoulders globed, globed like, globed and full
quote_en: "My father worked with a horse-plough, / His shoulders globed like a full sail strung / Between the shafts and the furrow. / The horses strained at his clicking tongue." – The speaker, remembering his childhood, describes his father's massive shoulders curved with effort like a wind-filled sail (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney coins 'globed' from the noun 'globe', transforming his father's shoulders into a world. The word is a verb of magnitude: it makes the body planetary, suggesting a figure so large and powerful that he occupies a different scale of being. The nautical simile that follows — 'like a full sail strung' — extends the sense of harnessed force, the father's body catching the wind of labour and converting it into forward motion. But 'globed' also suggests enclosure and completeness: a globe is a self-contained world, and the father, absorbed in his work, is a complete being, needing nothing beyond the horse, the plough, the furrow. The hard 'g' and plosive 'b' sounds give the word physical weight, and the past tense places it in memory — this is how the father once was, a figure of mythic proportions seen through the lens of a child's awe. The word establishes the poem's central technique: ordinary farm labour described with a vocabulary that reaches for the sublime. 'Globed' elevates ploughing into cosmology.
ex_en: The father's shoulders curved like a globe, full and round like a sail stretched tight with wind between the shafts of the plough.
ex_zh: My father's anger never globed like that — / he folded it small and square, / tucked it into the pocket of his overalls, / and ploughed it under the potato drills.

def_en: a person with comprehensive and authoritative knowledge of or skill in a particular area
def_zh: an expert, expert hand, expert eye
quote_en: "An expert. He would set the wing / And fit the bright steel-pointed sock. / The sod rolled over without breaking. / At the headrig, with a single pluck" – The speaker declares his father's mastery with a single-word sentence before describing the precise adjustments he made to the plough (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney isolates 'expert' as a one-word sentence, and the full stop after it is a gesture of absolute conviction. The word is not qualified or explained; it is simply stated, as if it were an objective fact of the universe. 'Expert' comes from the Latin 'expertus', meaning tried, tested, proven by experience, and the father's expertise is exactly this — knowledge held in the body, in the hands that set the wing and fit the sock. Heaney chooses 'expert' over 'skilled' or 'masterful' because the word carries a connotation of formal, almost scientific authority, yet it is applied to a farmer working a horse-plough. The contrast between the Latinate abstraction and the earthy, specific nouns that follow — 'wing', 'sock', 'sod', 'headrig' — enacts the poem's marriage of the elevated and the grounded. The father is an expert not in theory but in practice, in the intimate dialogue between body, tool, and earth. The word also establishes the child's perspective: to a boy watching his father work, skill looks like magic, and 'expert' is the name for the magician.
ex_en: The father was an expert ploughman who could set the blade and fit the sharp steel point so perfectly that the soil turned without shattering.
ex_zh: Expertise was his way of loving — / he adjusted the carburettor the way other men / whispered into their wives' hair, / each small calibration a kind of devotion.

def_en: the act of charting or surveying an area; representing the features of a landscape with precision
def_zh: mapping the furrow, close mapping, mapping out
quote_en: "Of reins, the sweating team turned round / And back into the land. His eye / Narrowed and angled at the ground, / Mapping the furrow exactly." – The father's narrowed eye surveys the ground with the precision of a cartographer charting a landscape (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney's 'mapping' transforms the act of looking into an act of intellectual creation. The father does not merely gaze at the furrow; he maps it, a verb that implies measurement, record, and authority. Cartographers map territories and claim them for the mapmaker's culture; the father maps the field and claims it for cultivation. The word elevates agricultural labour into a form of knowledge production. 'Mapping' also involves abstraction — the conversion of physical land into a mental representation — and Heaney suggests that the father's expertise involves precisely this kind of cognitive transformation. His eye 'narrowed and angled', verbs of intense focus, before the present participle 'mapping' completes the line. The continuous form of the verb stretches the action through time: the mapping is ongoing, a sustained act of attention. The adverb 'exactly' that ends the stanza reinforces the cartographic precision. There is no approximation in the father's relationship with the land — every furrow is registered, every contour measured. The child who watches this mapping is witnessing a form of genius he cannot yet replicate.
ex_en: The father's narrowed eye moved across the ground like a surveyor's instrument, mapping the line of the furrow with absolute precision.
ex_zh: She spent her twilight years mapping / the country of her childhood — / each river a vein on the back of her hand, / each town a freckle whose name she whispered before sleep.

def_en: tripped or lost balance while walking; moved clumsily or with difficulty
def_zh: stumbled in, fell and stumbled, stumbled after
quote_en: "I stumbled in his hob-nailed wake, / Fell sometimes on the polished sod; / Sometimes he rode me on his back / Dipping and rising to his plod." – The child speaker describes his clumsy attempts to follow his father across the ploughed field (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney's 'stumbled' is the poem's first verb applied to the child, and it is a word of failure and awkwardness, set in deliberate contrast to the father's sureness. Where the father globed and mapped and plucked, the son stumbles. The word carries the physical weight of a child's body not yet under control — tripping over clods, falling on the 'polished sod'. But 'stumbled' also carries an emotional meaning: to stumble in someone's wake is to follow inadequately, to fail to live up to the model ahead. The 'hob-nailed wake' is a brilliant compression: the father's heavy boots leave literal marks in the earth, and the son follows those tracks as a boat follows the wake of a great ship. The word 'wake' also hints at funeral rites, the watch kept over the dead, and this submerged meaning will surface in the poem's final stanzas when the roles reverse. The stumble is redeemed, briefly, by the father's tenderness — 'Sometimes he rode me on his back' — and the son's clumsiness is absorbed into the father's rhythm, 'dipping and rising to his plod'.
ex_en: The boy stumbled clumsily in the tracks left by his father's heavy boots, sometimes falling onto the freshly turned, gleaming earth.
ex_zh: I stumbled upon the truth of him / years after the funeral, / in a shoebox of receipts / where every kindness had been carefully itemised.

def_en: a dark area produced by a body blocking light; the constant accompaniment of a follower; a diminished version of something greater
def_zh: broad shadow, follow in his shadow, shadow round the farm
quote_en: "I wanted to grow up and plough, / To close one eye, stiffen my arm. / All I ever did was follow / In his broad shadow round the farm." – The boy admits that his ambition to become like his father resulted only in trailing behind him, always in the darkness he cast (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney's 'shadow' is the poem's central metaphor for inheritance, aspiration, and limitation. The shadow is 'broad', a word that testifies to the father's substance, the sheer physical reality of his presence that blocks the light. To follow in a shadow is to be close to greatness, protected and sheltered, but also to be obscured, never fully illuminated, never fully seen as oneself. The child's desire — to 'close one eye, stiffen my arm' — is a heartbreakingly precise imitation of the father's posture, but all he achieves is to walk in the darkness the father casts. 'Shadow' also carries a Platonic resonance: the shadow on the cave wall, the imperfect copy of a more real form. The son is a shadow of the father, a diminished version. But Heaney plants a further complexity: shadows shift as the sun moves. The shadow that falls behind the father in the morning will fall ahead of him in the evening. The word prepares, without the reader yet knowing it, for the role reversal of the final stanza, where the father will stumble in the son's shadow.
ex_en: The boy could only ever follow in the broad dark shadow his father cast as he walked around the farm, never stepping out into his own light.
ex_zh: His shadow was a country I was born in — / I knew every hill, every hollow, / every place the sun did not reach, / but I never learned the language of its borders.

def_en: tripping or losing balance repeatedly; moving unsteadily; faltering
def_zh: keeps stumbling, comes stumbling, stumbling behind
quote_en: "I was a nuisance, tripping, falling, / Yapping always. But today / It is my father who keeps stumbling / Behind me, and will not go away." – The speaker reveals that time has reversed their roles; now it is the aged father who stumbles behind the adult son he once carried (Follower, 1966)
quote_zh: Heaney closes with 'stumbling', an echo of the earlier 'stumbled' that transfers the verb from son to father with devastating economy. For four stanzas, 'stumbling' has been the child's word, the mark of his inadequacy. Its reappearance, now attached to the father, is a shock that forces the reader to re-evaluate everything that preceded it. The present participle 'stumbling' is ongoing, durative — the father does not stumble once but 'keeps stumbling', a continuous present that will not end. The word is also progressive in a grammatical sense: the action is not completed, the father is still alive, still behind the son, still 'stumbling'. The irony is that the son, who once aspired to be like his father, has now become the steady one, the one who walks ahead. But there is no triumph in this reversal. The father 'will not go away', and the phrase is ambiguous — is it the persistence of love or the burden of care? 'Stumbling' drains the father of the grandeur Heaney had so carefully built. The expert who mapped the furrow, whose shoulders globed like a sail, is now unsteady on his feet, following his son as the son once followed him. The word is a quiet, devastating act of witness — the son who could not plough the field must now navigate a different kind of difficult ground.
ex_en: Now, in the present day, it is the elderly father who keeps stumbling behind his grown son, his steps unsteady, refusing to be left behind.
ex_zh: The years came stumbling after him / like children who had lost their shoes — / barefoot and bleeding at the heel, / still trying to catch up with the man he used to be.

def_en: a goddess of destiny in classical mythology; the power that predetermines the course of events
def_zh: like a fate, spinning fate, the three Fates
quote_en: "She sat, like a fate, spinning the yarn, / When did she say it was? / The storyteller. The one who held the end / Of the thread, who cut and bound." – The speaker compares the storyteller to one of the mythological Fates who spin, measure, and sever the thread of human life (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead opens with 'fate', a word that instantly elevates the storyteller from a mere entertainer to a figure of cosmic power. In Greek mythology, the three Fates — Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos — controlled the span of every human life: one spun the thread, one measured it, one cut it. By comparing the storyteller to a Fate, Lochhead claims that the one who tells stories also controls destinies — she spins the yarn (both the wool and the tale), holds the end of the thread, and decides where to cut. The word 'fate' also carries its common meaning of inevitable destiny, and the poem suggests that stories are a form of fate: once you enter a story, once you are held by it, your attention and your emotion are no longer your own. The storyteller 'cut and bound' — two verbs of absolute finality, the authority to end and to secure. The opening line's comma-separated simile — 'like a fate' — sets the storyteller slightly apart from full identification with the goddesses; she is like a fate, a mortal woman performing a divine function.
ex_en: The storyteller sat like one of the Fates from ancient myth, spinning thread and tale alike, holding the power to begin and end the story as she chose.
ex_zh: My grandmother spun fate from a kitchen chair, / her knitting needles clicking out the lifelines / of everyone she'd ever loved — / she never dropped a stitch, and she never forgave a dropped stitch.

def_en: crowded together closely, especially for warmth, comfort, or protection from danger
def_zh: huddled by the fire, huddled together, huddled close
quote_en: "The circle of the children would draw in close. / They huddled by the fire. / The gates of the dark were closed; / They were safe in here" – The children press together in a tight group around the hearth, protected from the darkness outside by fire and story (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead's 'huddled' is a word of vulnerability and communion. It describes a physical posture — bodies drawn inward, shoulders rounded, limbs pulled close — that is both protective and expressive of need. The children huddle by the fire, and the verb suggests that the fire is not merely a source of warmth but a refuge, a small point of light in a surrounding darkness. 'Huddled' also carries a faintly animal quality, as if the children are a litter of creatures seeking warmth from the mother-body of the storyteller. The word establishes the poem's spatial and emotional geometry: there is a circle, with the storyteller at its centre or its edge, and inside the circle there is safety, and outside there are 'wolves, tigers, bears'. The 'gates of the dark' are closed, a fairy-tale image that transforms the ordinary room into a fortress. 'Huddled' is the bodily manifestation of that safety — the children do not merely sit; they huddle, as if the dark might seep through the cracks and must be physically blocked by their gathered bodies.
ex_en: The children drew in close around the fire, huddled together for warmth and safety as the darkness was shut out beyond the room.
ex_zh: We huddled around my father's silences / like travellers around a dying campfire — / each of us cupping our hands / around a flame that had forgotten how to give light.

def_en: a form of words used as a magical charm or incantation; a state of enchantment; also, a period of time
def_zh: hold in a spell, cast a spell, under her spell
quote_en: "With her, who could hold them in her spell, / Could weave the threads and spin the yarn / And pull them by the ears. / Safe from the wolves, the tigers, the bears," – The storyteller possesses the power to enchant the children, holding them captive in the magic of her words (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead exploits the double meaning of 'spell' with extraordinary economy. A spell is both a magical enchantment and the act of forming words from letters — to spell a word is to build it from the alphabet's raw material. The storyteller holds the children 'in her spell', and the phrase means both that they are enchanted and that they are held inside language itself, inside the spelled-out words of the story. This double meaning is the poem's central insight: storytelling is a form of magic, and magic is a form of literacy. The verb 'hold' is important — the spell is a container, a bounded space of attention. The storyteller can also 'pull them by the ears', a phrase that suggests both the physical tug of a parent and the way a story literally enters through hearing, through the ears. 'Spell' is followed by a list of predators — 'wolves, tigers, bears' — and the implication is that the spell is what keeps these dangers at bay. When the spell breaks, the beasts of the ordinary world will return. The word also anticipates the poem's later coinage 'unspelled' — the state of being released from enchantment.
ex_en: The storyteller could hold the children inside her spell, enchanting them with woven words while she tugged at their ears and kept every danger beyond the firelight.
ex_zh: The spell of his attention was so complete / that the room forgot its own dimensions — / the walls leaned in to listen, / and the clock stopped counting the minutes she was not there.

def_en: a single distinct meaningful element of speech or writing; the fundamental unit of language
def_zh: a word then another, power of the word, spoken word
quote_en: "As long as there was wood to burn, a turn / Of the page, a log shifted and fell / On the dying fire. / A word, then another, then another." – The speaker describes the rhythm of storytelling as a sequence of single words building one upon another, like logs feeding a fire (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead places 'word' at the centre of a ritualistic repetition — 'A word, then another, then another' — that enacts the very process it describes. The sentence is itself a sequence of words, each one succeeding the last, building meaning through accumulation. 'Word' is the fundamental unit of the storyteller's art, and Lochhead reduces the magic of narrative to this simplest of elements: one word after another, a chain of sound and sense. The parallel image of the fire — 'a log shifted and fell / On the dying fire' — links words to fuel, to the material that sustains warmth and light. As long as there are words, the fire of the story does not go out. But the fire is 'dying', and the participle is ominous: words can run out, the log can burn to ash, the story can end. 'Word' is also, in the Christian tradition, the name for the divine Logos — 'In the beginning was the Word' — and Lochhead may be glancing at this resonance, suggesting that the storyteller's words are a secular creation myth, bringing worlds into being. The triple repetition — 'another, then another' — is a spell in itself, an incantation that keeps the darkness at bay.
ex_en: The story continued as long as there was fuel for the fire, and the storyteller kept adding one word after another after another to sustain the light.
ex_zh: Each word she spoke was a small stone / dropped into the well of the room — / we listened for the splash / and counted the ripples spreading through the dark.

def_en: a set of rules or principles governing a particular sphere; the structural conventions of a tradition
def_zh: old laws, laws of the tale, fairy-tale laws
quote_en: "Saltportal, steeplecup, storyville. / The old laws of the tale. The iron shoes. / The witch's house baked like gingerbread, / The children in the wood." – The speaker invokes the deep, unchanging rules that govern the fairy-tale universe (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead's 'laws' is a striking word to apply to fairy tales, which might seem, to the casual reader, to be a realm of pure fantasy and caprice. But 'laws' insists that the fairy-tale world has a structure as rigorous as any legal code. The old laws dictate that iron shoes will be heated in fires, that witches live in houses made of gingerbread, that children will be lost in woods. These are the narrative equivalents of precedent, the binding rules that govern what can and cannot happen in a story. The word 'old' is crucial: these laws predate the storyteller, predate the children, predate the poem itself. They belong to the long tradition of oral storytelling, and the storyteller is not their inventor but their custodian. 'Laws' also carries a faintly threatening quality — laws can be broken, and breaking them has consequences. The three preceding words — 'Saltportal, steeplecup, storyville' — are invented place-names, magical toponyms that suggest entry points into the tale. But 'laws' pulls the poem back from whimsy into structure, reminding the reader that the freedom of story operates within strict and ancient boundaries. The fairy tale is not anarchy; it is a different kind of order.
ex_en: The old laws of the fairy tale governed everything — iron shoes for the wicked, gingerbread houses in the forest, and children always wandering too far from home.
ex_zh: The old laws of our family were unwritten and absolute — / you never asked about the war, / you never mentioned her name, / and you always passed the salt with your right hand.

def_en: a solemn utterance intended to invoke supernatural harm; a great affliction or misfortune; the state of being under an evil spell
def_zh: under a curse, break the curse, cast a curse
quote_en: "This was a country under a curse / Where cinder lads won princesses, and tinker lads / Cut switches for their weeping sisters, / And there was always a talking bird, a glass hill," – The speaker describes the fairy-tale landscape as a place afflicted by an enchantment that must be broken (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead's 'curse' defines the fairy-tale world as a place of suffering from which transformation is possible. A curse, unlike a mere problem or misfortune, is a supernatural affliction that demands a supernatural solution. The country under a curse is the country of every fairy tale: a place where things are wrong, where the natural order has been disrupted, and where the story's task is to restore justice. 'Curse' also carries the weight of its Old English root — 'curs' — meaning a solemn utterance, a prayer for harm. The storyteller who speaks the curse into the story is, in a sense, its author, but she is also the one who will break it through the story's resolution. The line that follows — 'cinder lads won princesses' — is a compressed version of Cinderella reversed, and the 'tinker lads' cutting switches for weeping sisters belong to another tale entirely. Lochhead's 'curse' is the condition that sets all these stories in motion: poverty, oppression, grief, injustice. But the curse is also what makes the story possible; without it, there is no narrative, no quest, no transformation. The 'talking bird' and 'glass hill' that close the stanza are promises that the curse can be broken, that the tale has the resources to undo what has been done.
ex_en: The fairy-tale country lay under a curse where poor boys won princesses, wandering boys comforted their grieving sisters, and strange wonders waited around every corner.
ex_zh: The curse on our house was silence — / not the peaceful kind, / but the kind that screamed at 3 a.m. / in the key of a question no one was allowed to ask.

def_en: a place where plays are performed; dramatic performance as an art form; a space where significant events are enacted
def_zh: theatre of memory, living theatre, theatre of the mind
quote_en: "A silver nut, a nutmeg tree, / A wise woman in a high tower. / This was the start of it. The theatre / Of memory. The world. The word." – The speaker names storytelling as a performance played out on the stage of memory itself (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead's 'theatre' reframes storytelling as performance, as a spatial and visual art as well as a verbal one. A theatre is a place of seeing (from the Greek 'theatron', a place for viewing), and the word insists that the story is not merely heard but witnessed, that the children are not passive listeners but an audience watching a drama unfold in their imaginations. 'Theatre of memory' is a phrase of extraordinary compression: memory itself becomes a stage, and the stories that are told become the actors that walk upon it. Lochhead may be alluding to the Renaissance concept of the memory theatre — a mental architecture for organising knowledge — but she makes it a space for narrative rather than information. The sequence that follows — 'The world. The word.' — is a series of identifications: theatre is memory is world is word. The chain of noun sentences creates a logic of equivalence: the storyteller's word contains the world, and the world is performed in the theatre of memory, and memory is what the story builds and occupies. The enjambment that splits 'theatre' from 'of memory' across the line break enacts a brief pause, a curtain rising, before the phrase completes itself. 'Theatre' also suggests a degree of artifice, of conscious construction — the story is made, not merely remembered, and the storyteller is its director.
ex_en: The story began here, in the theatre of memory where the world itself was conjured from the storyteller's single commanding word.
ex_zh: Her mind had become a theatre of memory / where the same scene played nightly — / a woman at a window, a road in autumn, / and a car that never quite arrived.

def_en: the ability or capacity to do something; control or influence over others; authority
def_zh: power was hers, hold the power, gift of power
quote_en: "Power was hers because she had to pass / From winter into spring, then summer, then the fall. / The words to do with beast, / To do with tree and stone and the weather." – The speaker declares that the storyteller's authority comes from her knowledge of seasonal cycles and her command of elemental language (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead places 'power' at the head of a line, capitalising it by position, and the word is the poem's most direct statement of what the storyteller possesses. But this power is not arbitrary or tyrannical; it derives from the storyteller's alignment with natural cycles — 'winter into spring, then summer, then the fall'. She has power because she knows the rhythm of the year, the pattern of growth and decay, the deep time of seasons. 'Power' is connected to the most ancient kinds of knowledge: 'beast', 'tree', 'stone', 'weather' — a lexicon of the pre-literate, the pre-modern, the elemental. Lochhead chooses these four nouns with care: beast for the animal, tree for the living plant, stone for the mineral earth, weather for the moving sky. The storyteller has the words for all of them, and this vocabulary is her power. The word also carries a feminist resonance — a woman's power, a power that is not political or military but imaginative and linguistic, passed down through the oral tradition. The storyteller 'had to pass' through the seasons, and the phrase suggests both the necessity of the natural cycle and the storyteller's role as one who passes knowledge from one generation to the next.
ex_en: The storyteller's power came from her passage through the turning seasons and her command of the ancient words for beast and tree, stone and weather.
ex_zh: Her power was not in the thunderclap / but in the quiet after — / the way she could sit in a room / and let the silence do all the shouting.

def_en: released from a spell or enchantment; returned to ordinary, unmagical consciousness
def_zh: went unspelled, left unspelled, children unspelled
quote_en: "She could bring them back. The words / Were hers. The words were all the gift she had. / Love, spells, both. Though the next day, / The children went unspelled. They had to learn," – The speaker reveals that after the story ends, the children are released from enchantment and must face the ordinary world again (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead coins 'unspelled' by adding the negating prefix 'un-' to 'spelled', creating a word that means released from a spell, disenchanted, returned to ordinary consciousness. The coinage is audacious because there is no standard English verb 'to spell' meaning 'to enchant' that can be negated this way — but Lochhead's invention works because the poem has already established the double meaning of 'spell' as both magic and language. To be 'unspelled' is to have the words taken away, to be stripped of the enchantment that held you. The word marks the boundary between the story-world and the day-world, between the firelit circle and the morning after. 'The children went unspelled' — the verb 'went' suggests a change of state, a crossing of a threshold. The word also carries a submerged pun on 'unspooled', as if the children are thread unwinding from the storyteller's spindle. The 'spell' that held them was a temporary enchantment, and its breaking is inevitable — the story must end, the fire must die, the children must return to 'Sticks and stones and the kick in the head'. 'Unspelled' is the poem's most poignant word because it names the loss that follows every ending, the silence after the last word.
ex_en: The next morning, the children walked into the daylight unspelled, the magic of the story dissolved, forced to face the ordinary savagery of the waking world.
ex_zh: After she left, the house stood unspelled — / the kettle forgot how to whistle, / the mirror showed only faces, / and the cat stopped talking to the moon.

def_en: the quality of being fierce, violent, and uncontrolled; brutal cruelty; the state of being uncivilised
def_zh: savagery of the day, daily savagery, raw savagery
quote_en: "After the ending of the story, there was the rest of their lives. / Sticks and stones and the kick in the head, / The cockcrow at morning, the savagery of the day. / No glass mountain. No magic inside the words." – The speaker contrasts the enchanted world of the story with the brutal reality that awaits the children after the tale concludes (Storyteller, 1981)
quote_zh: Lochhead closes with 'savagery', the harshest word in the poem and the precise opposite of the spell that has sustained the story's enchantment. 'Savagery' is from the French 'sauvage', meaning wild, untamed, belonging to the forest — and the word suggests that the ordinary day is a wilderness more threatening than any fairy-tale wood. The 'cockcrow at morning' is a biblical echo, Peter's betrayal of Christ, and it frames the new day as a scene of disenchantment and failure. 'Savagery' names what the story had kept at bay: the cruelty of other children ('sticks and stones'), the violence of adults ('the kick in the head'), the indifferent brutality of the ordinary. The word is deliberately excessive — a day is not ordinarily savage — and the excess makes the point: to a child who has been held in the spell of a story, the world outside that spell feels like an assault. 'No glass mountain. No magic inside the words.' The double negation is absolute. The glass mountain was the fairy-tale obstacle, the impossible challenge that could be overcome; its absence means there is no quest, no transformation, only the flat and unenchanted ground of the ordinary. 'Savagery' is the final word on the limits of story: the spell cannot last, and when it breaks, the world is waiting with its sticks and stones.
ex_en: After the story finished, the children had to face the rest of their lives — the raw savagery of an ordinary day with no glass mountain and no magic left inside the words.
ex_zh: Savagery, I learned, was not the wolf at the door / but the postman whistling through his rounds, / the kettle boiling for tea at five, / the terrible, unbearable normalcy of everything continuing.

def_en: assures someone that one will do something or that something will happen; gives grounds for expecting
def_zh: promises a new day, warm promises, empty promises
quote_en: "The early rising sun / promises a new day. / You can feel it / in the fresh, gentle stir of the air / playing among the wet leaves / and green, green grass." – The speaker observes the dawn sun offering its daily assurance of renewal, felt in the soft movement of the morning air (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi opens with 'promises', a verb that personifies the sun as a maker of commitments and establishes the poem's central tension between the natural world's cycles and the isolated human heart. A promise is a speech act, something that requires language and intention, and by attributing it to the sun, the speaker frames nature as a benevolent communicator. The word is repeated twice in the opening movement — the sun 'promises a new day', the air carries 'warm promises' — building an atmosphere of trust and expectation. But a promise can be broken, and the word's shadow meaning is already present: promises are made precisely because the future is uncertain. The sensory evidence for the promise — the 'fresh, gentle stir of the air', the 'wet leaves', the doubled 'green, green grass' — is rendered with a simplicity that borders on the naive. This simplicity will be shattered by the 'But' that opens the second stanza. 'Promises' sets the poem up as a dialogue between the external world's assurances and the internal world's desolation. The sun promises what it cannot deliver to this particular speaker.
ex_en: The early morning sun promises the start of a new day, and you can feel this assurance in the gentle movement of the air through the wet leaves and the vivid green grass.
ex_zh: The morning promised nothing it could keep — / its light was borrowed from a bankrupt sky, / its warmth was the memory of warmth, / and its birds sang only in a language of goodbye.

def_en: never descending below the horizon; never ending or resting; a sun that cannot go down
def_zh: un-setting sun, un-setting pain, un-setting light
quote_en: "But this early pearly dawn / promises no day / for the rising, un-setting sun / within my head." – The speaker reveals that the external dawn offers no hope because an internal sun blazes endlessly inside the mind, a consciousness that cannot find rest (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi coins 'un-setting' by negating the natural behaviour of the sun, and the word is the poem's most concentrated image of psychological torment. A sun that sets brings night, darkness, rest, the possibility of sleep and oblivion. An 'un-setting sun' is a consciousness condemned to permanent wakefulness, a burning at the centre of the self that will not go down. The hyphenated form holds two words in tension — 'un' as negation, 'setting' as the ritual of ending — and the compound becomes a miniature drama of refusal. The word is placed in direct contrast with the 'early pearly dawn' of the natural world, and the juxtaposition is devastating: outside, the sun is just rising, gentle and promising; inside, a different sun has been blazing without pause, without mercy. 'Un-setting' also carries a submerged echo of 'unsettling' — the experience of being disturbed, made anxious, deprived of peace. Mungoshi, a Zimbabwean poet writing against the backdrop of colonialism and personal exile, uses 'un-setting' to describe a mind that cannot settle, cannot rest, cannot find the darkness it needs to survive. The rising external sun, which should bring hope, only illuminates the fact that the internal sun has been burning all night.
ex_en: Inside the speaker's head, a sun burns without ever setting, offering neither the darkness of night nor any promise of rest from its relentless light.
ex_zh: His grief was an un-setting sun — / it hung at noon all night, / bleaching the sheets, / turning sleep into a desert of white hours.

def_en: free from moral wrong; not guilty; without experience of evil or suffering; naive
def_zh: innocent sky, innocent eyes, innocent blue
quote_en: "An innocent blue sky / opens her eyes, ready for the warm promises / of the coming sun." – The speaker personifies the morning sky as a trusting, blameless creature waking to receive the sun's assurances (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi's 'innocent' is an aching word, a judgement passed on the natural world from the vantage point of suffering. The sky is innocent because it does not know what the speaker knows — it has not suffered, it has not lain awake through the night with an un-setting sun in its head. The word carries both its primary meaning (free from guilt, blameless) and its secondary meaning (naive, inexperienced, unaware of evil). The sky 'opens her eyes' like a child waking, 'ready for the warm promises' — and the phrase is heartbreaking because the speaker knows that the promises will not be kept, or will be kept only for the sky, not for him. 'Innocent' also carries a theological resonance: the state before the Fall, the condition of Eden before knowledge brought suffering. The sky lives in a prelapsarian world, and the speaker has been exiled from it. The feminine pronoun 'her' for the sky adds a tenderness that makes the speaker's exclusion more painful — the sky is a beloved, innocent being from whom he is separated by the 'trouble of his soul'. The word divides the poem's universe: the innocent sky and the guilty, knowing, suffering speaker.
ex_en: The clear blue sky was as innocent as a child just waking, opening its eyes to receive the warm promises the rising sun was about to make.
ex_zh: The innocent morning stretched its light / across the floor like a cat / who did not know that the night had died / and that the house was in mourning.

def_en: containing nothing; vacant; lacking meaning, substance, or feeling
def_zh: empty sky, empty promises, cold and empty
quote_en: "But this pale, empty, waiting sky / promises no warmth / for the coming of the day is no coming at all." – The speaker turns again from the external world, finding the dawn sky void of the comfort it seems to offer (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi's 'empty' is the condition that negates all the promises the poem has accumulated. The sky that was 'innocent' and 'ready' is now 'pale, empty, waiting' — three adjectives that drain it of the warmth it seemed to possess. 'Empty' is a word of absolute negation: to be empty is to have nothing inside, to be a vessel without contents, a space without presence. The sky waits, but it waits for nothing, because 'the coming of the day is no coming at all'. The phrase is a philosophical negation — the day arrives, the sun rises, but for the speaker, arrival is indistinguishable from absence. 'Empty' also describes the speaker's own soul, which will be named in the next stanza as a place of 'trouble'. The word links the external sky and the internal landscape: both are voids, both are waiting, both are without warmth. Mungoshi's repetition of 'promises no' — earlier 'promises no day', here 'promises no warmth' — creates a ritual of negation, a litany of refusals that systematically strips the dawn of every comforting meaning. The sky is pale, not bright; empty, not full; waiting, not receiving. And the speaker waits too, but for what? For the un-setting sun to finally set, for the trouble to end, for the darkness to come.
ex_en: The sky looked pale and empty as it waited, offering no promise of warmth because the arrival of the new day felt like no arrival at all.
ex_zh: The empty chair at the table / grew heavier each year — / it absorbed every silence, / and by the end, it weighed more than anyone sitting.

def_en: difficulty or problems; a state of distress, disturbance, or inner turmoil; spiritual affliction
def_zh: trouble of my soul, deep trouble, inner trouble
quote_en: "In the trouble of my soul / more than the sun rises / more than the sun sets." – The speaker finally names his condition directly: a deep disturbance in the soul that exceeds the natural rhythms of sunrise and sunset (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi chooses 'trouble' after building the poem's entire architecture of natural imagery, and the word's deliberate plainness is its power. 'Trouble' is an ordinary word, a word for daily difficulties — a child is in trouble, a machine has trouble, a plan runs into trouble. But Mungoshi applies it to 'my soul', and the ordinary becomes profound. The phrase 'trouble of my soul' echoes the language of the Psalms — 'Why art thou cast down, O my soul, and why art thou disquieted within me?' — and invokes a tradition of spiritual lament that stretches back thousands of years. 'Trouble' is the word the speaker offers after all the metaphors of sun and sky have been exhausted, and its simplicity is a form of honesty. The parallel structure of the lines that follow — 'more than the sun rises / more than the sun sets' — measures the trouble against the cosmic cycle and finds the cosmos wanting. The trouble of the soul exceeds the natural world; it is larger than sunrise, more enduring than sunset. The repetition of 'more than' is an accumulation, a stacking-up of inner anguish against the external world's indifference. The sun will rise and set endlessly, but the trouble of the soul is, for the speaker, greater than all of it.
ex_en: Inside the speaker's soul is a trouble so vast that it surpasses even the great rhythms of the rising and setting sun.
ex_zh: The trouble had no name — / it sat in the corner of his chest, / a small grey stone that grew heavier / each time he pretended it wasn't there.

def_en: producing a prolonged high-pitched cry of grief, pain, or rage; lamenting audibly
def_zh: wailing spirits, wailing wind, wailing cry
quote_en: "Do not go down. / Do not. / Do not leave me cold and dead, empty, silent, / with the wailing spirits of the dark." – The speaker pleads with the internal sun not to abandon him to the darkness and the howling ghosts that wait there (Before the Sun, 1971)
quote_zh: Mungoshi closes with 'wailing', a word that fills the poem's final line with the sound of grief. 'Wailing' is the audible manifestation of mourning — the high, keening cry that has been part of human and animal lament since before language. By placing 'wailing spirits' in the 'dark' that will come if the inner sun sets, Mungoshi transforms the darkness from a potential relief into a place of terror. The speaker has spent the poem describing an un-setting sun as torment, but now he begs it not to go down — because what waits in the darkness is worse. The paradox is the poem's final truth: the speaker cannot live with the burning sun, but he cannot live without it either; the darkness is populated by 'spirits', by voices that wail.
